A considerable cross-cultural interchange between the Persian Empire and the thriving Vijayanagara Empire in South India occurred during this diplomatic encounter in the 15th century. The creation of diplomatic and commercial links between the two regions was made possible by Abdur Razzaq's visit. His views and writings contributed significantly to our understanding of the political, social, and cultural dimensions of the Vijayanagara Kingdom. These narratives provide insight into the vibrant and highly developed civilization that flourished in Vijayanagara at its height. Hence, the correct answer is "Deva Raya II".

q: Tansen, who was included in the Navaratnas of Akbar, was an expert in playing which of the following musical instruments?
subject: history
exp: Tansen, one of Akbar's Navaratnas (nine gems), was a legendary figure in Indian music. He was a prominent composer and musician in the court of Emperor Akbar during the Mughal era. Tansen is widely recognized for his exceptional skills in Hindustani classical music. Tansen's artistry was not limited to vocal performances; he was also an expert in playing various musical instruments, including the rabab. His famous compositions like Miyan ki Todi and Miyan ki Malhar continue to be revered. Hence, the correct answer is "Rabab".

q: Who among the following was the founder of the Bahmani Sultanate, who took the title of Bahman Shah after he acceded to the throne?
subject: history
exp: The Bahmani Sultanate, also known as the Bahmani Empire, was established in 1347. It was founded by a Turkish general named Alauddin Bahman Shah. He initiated a revolt against Mohammed bin Tughlaq, the ruler of the Delhi Sultanate at that time. After he acceded to the throne, Alauddin Bahman Shah adopted the title of Bahman Shah. He chose Kalaburgi (or Gulbarga) in Karnataka as the capital of the Bahmani Sultanate. Under his leadership, the Bahmani Sultanate became a prominent power in the Deccan region, reigning for almost two centuries. Eventually, the Bahmani Sultanate fragmented into five successor states, collectively known as the Deccan Sultanates. Hence, the correct answer is "Alauddin Hasan."

q: Muhammad bin Tughlaq introduced a copper coin called ______ in place of the silver coin.
subject: history
exp: There was a shortage of silver in the world in the fourteenth century and India had to face the crisis. Therefore, the Sultan was forced to issue copper coins instead of silver. He introduced a copper coin (Jittal) instead of a silver coin (tanka) and ordered that it be accepted as equivalent to the tanka. However, the idea of token currency was new in India and was difficult for traders and common people to accept. Hence, the correct answer is "Jittal."

q: Name the Bhakti Saint from South India who was initially a Jaina and a minister in the court of a Chalukya king in the twelfth century.
subject: history
exp: Basavanna, also known as Basava, is a prominent Bhakti Saint and philosopher from South India who lived in the 12th century. He is considered one of the founding figures of the Lingayat sect, a distinct Shaivite religious tradition. Basavanna's life and teachings have had a profound impact on the socio-religious landscape of southern India, particularly in the states of Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh, and Telangana. He was born into a Brahmin family and was initially a follower of Jainism. He served as a minister in the court of King Bijjala of the Chalukya dynasty, where he held a high administrative position. Hence, the correct answer is “Basavanna”.

q: In which of the following cities is the Quwwat al-Islam mosque situated?
subject: history
exp: The Quwwat al-Islam Mosque, often referred to simply as the Qutb Mosque, is a historical mosque located in Delhi, India. It was constructed in the late 12th century by Qutb-ud-din Aibak, the founder of the Delhi Sultanate. The mosque is known for its impressive Indo-Islamic architectural style. It incorporates elements of Indian architecture, such as intricate floral and geometric patterns, with Islamic features like the use of arches, domes, and pillars. The Quwwat al-Islam Mosque is part of a larger complex that includes the iconic Qutb Minar, a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Hence, the correct answer is “Delhi”.

q: Who composed 'Tabaqat-i-Nasiri' in the Delhi Sultanate period?
subject: history
exp: Minhaj-i-Siraj was a 13th-century Persian historian. He wrote the book titled "Tabaqat-i- Nasiri". This exceptional work serves as a crucial source of information on India's history, spanning from the era of the Ghaznavid dynasty to the early years of the Delhi Sultanate. It details the different 'tabaqat' (classes or categories) of rulers during the Nasiri dynasty's reign, which is why it is called "Tabaqat-i-Nasiri". The chronicle recounts a range of sociopolitical events and offers valuable insights into the diverse cultures and societies of that time. Hence, the correct answer is "Minhaj-i- Siraj".

q: Krishnadeva Raya composed a work on _______ in Telugu known as the Amuktamalyada.
subject: history
exp: The most well-known Vijayanagara ruler, Krishnadevaraya, wrote the Amuktamalyada, a Telugu treatise on statecraft. Amuktamalyada tells the story of the wedding of the Hindu Lord Ranganayaka, an avatar of Vishnu, and Goda Devi, the Tamil Alvar poet and daughter of Periyalvar, at Srirangam. From 1509 until 1530, Krishnadevaraya ruled as the king of the Vijayanagara Empire. He was the Tuluva Dynasty's third ruler and presided over the Vijayanagara empire at its peak. Krishnadevaraya constructed massive irrigation tanks and reservoirs to collect rainwater. He gave the money he won from his battles to all the main South Indian temples so they could build temple gateways (gopuras), known as "Rayagopuram," in his honor. Hence, the correct answer is "statecraft".

q: Which of the following systems of the Delhi sultanate had a influence on the Bahmani and Vijayanagar kingdoms?
subject: history
exp: The Iqtadari system was a land revenue and administration system used by the Delhi Sultanate. It was also adopted by the Bahmani and Vijayanagara kingdoms. Under the Iqtadari system, the sultan granted land to military officers and nobles in return for military service and revenue collection. The Iqtadars, as these officers and nobles were known, were responsible for collecting taxes from the peasants and maintaining law and order in their assigned areas. Hence,the correct answer is "Iqtadari".

q: Which officer under Alauddin Khilji was required to maintain a register of merchants to ensure an adequate supply of goods?
subject: history
exp: The Shahna-i-Mandi was an important administrative officer under the rule of Alauddin Khilji, responsible for regulating markets and trade in the kingdom. This officer maintained a register of merchants to monitor and ensure a steady supply of goods, which was crucial for maintaining market stability and preventing price fluctuations. The role of Shahna-i-Mandi included overseeing the quality and quantity of goods in the market, thereby enhancing trade efficiency. This position was part of Alauddin Khilji's broader economic reforms aimed at controlling prices and managing resources effectively to support his military campaigns and urban populations. The introduction of such regulatory measures was significant in shaping the economic landscape during the Khilji dynasty. Hence, the correct answer is "Shahna-i- Mandi."

q: Who among the following was defeated in the battle of Chanderi in 1528?
subject: history
exp: The Battle of Chanderi took place in 1528 between the forces of the Mughal Emperor Babur and the combined army of Medini Rai, the ruler of Chanderi, and the Afghan chief Mahmud Lodi. Babur established his rule in parts of North India after his victory at the First Battle of Panipat in 1526. He aimed to expand his empire further by securing control over the region and defeating various local rulers. Babur's forces consisted of a relatively small but disciplined and well-trained army. On the other side, Medini Rai of Chanderi and Mahmud Lodi of Etawah formed an alliance to resist Babur's expansion. Babur's forces faced fierce resistance from the defenders of Chanderi, who were determined to protect their city. The battle was intense and prolonged, with both sides fighting fiercely. And the Babur defeated Medini Rai at last. Hence, the correct answer is "Medini Rai".

q: Under whose reign was the maximum number of books in Persian on classical Indian Music written?
subject: history
exp: During the reign of Aurangzeb, the maximum number of books in Persian on classical Indian music were written. Aurangzeb ruled the Mughal Empire from 1658 to 1707. Several research shows that Aurangzeb banished singing from his court, but not the playing of musical instruments. Moreover, he himself was an accomplished veena player. Music in all forms was patronized by his queens in the harem and by the nobles. Hence, the correct answer is "Aurangzeb".

q: Name the French jeweller who travelled to India at least six times during the Mughal period.
subject: history
exp: Jean-Baptiste Tavernier was a famous French jeweler and traveler who visited India multiple times during the Mughal period. Jean-Baptiste Tavernier embarked on six independent voyages to Persia and India between 1630 and 1668, covering an impressive distance of 60,000 leagues at his own expense. Antonio Monserrate was a Spanish missionary and traveler who visited the Mughal court during the reign of Akbar. Seydi Ali Reis (1498–1563) was a distinguished Ottoman navigator and admiral. He commanded the left wing of the Ottoman fleet during the significant naval battle of Preveza in 1538. Peter Mundy was an English traveler and merchant who visited India during the 17th century. Hence, the correct answer is “Jean-Baptiste Tavernier”.

q: While historians use the term Vijayanagara Empire and contemporaries of this empire described it as the_______________.
subject: history
exp: The Vijayanagara empire was described as the "Karnataka Samrajyamu." This term reflects the fact that the empire was centered in the Karnataka region of South India. The Vijayanagara empire was based in the Deccan, in peninsular and southern India, from 1336 onwards. It was founded by Harihara, also known as Hakka, and his brother Bukka Raya. It is named after its capital city (now ruined) of Vijayanagara, in modern Karnataka, India. Hence the correct answer is "Karnataka Samrajyamu".

q: During the reign of Akbar, _____was the land that has lain fallow for three or four years.
subject: history
exp: All of the lands used for cultivation in the empire were classified under four types - Polaj, Parauti, Chachar, and Banjar. Polaj land was always cultivated and was never left fallow. Parauti land was allowed to lie fallow for a year or two to recover its strength. Chachar land had to be left uncultivated for a period of three to four years, Hence, the correct answer is "Chachar".

q: The local communities of merchants were known as ______ in the Vijayanagara empire.
subject: history
exp: The Kudirai Chettis were the local communities of merchants in the Vijayanagara empire. They were a powerful and influential group, and they played a major role in the empire's economy. The Kudirai Chettis were responsible for trade and commerce, and they also provided financial support to the empire. The Kudirai Chettis were a sub-caste of the Vaisyas, one of the four main varnas in Hindu society. They were mainly involved in the trade of horses, but they also traded in other goods such as textiles, spices, and precious metals. The Kudirai Chettis had a well-developed network of trading routes that connected the Vijayanagara empire with other parts of India and the world. They were also involved in banking and lending, and they played an important role in the development of the empire's financial system. Hence, the correct answer is "Kudirai Chettis".

q: Who among the following was the slave- general of Alauddin Khilji, who led his army in the battle against Ramachandra of Devagiri?
subject: history
exp: The slave-general of Alauddin Khilji who led his army in the battle against Ramachandra of Devagiri was Malik Kafur. Malik Kafur was a prominent military commander and a trusted advisor of Alauddin Khilji, the second ruler of the Khilji dynasty in the Delhi Sultanate. He played a key role in several military campaigns and conquests during Khilji's reign. Malik Kafur led the Delhi Sultanate's forces in their campaign against Ramachandra of Devagiri. Alauddin Khilji was a notable ruler of the Delhi Sultanate in medieval India. He ruled from 1296 to 1316 and was the second ruler of the Khilji dynasty. Hence, the correct answer is "Malik Kafur".

q: Malik Ambar, who resisted Mughals in Deccan, was an able administrator of which state?
subject: history
exp: Malik Ambar was from central Ethiopia and was born as Chapu in 1548. He was a member of the Oromo community, who had recently arrived in the area due to ongoing conflict. The Deccan was a complex tapestry of conflict, political intrigue, and a precarious balance of various cultures and religions during Ambar's time. Ambar went to the Bijapur Sultanate in the south and joined its armed forces. He met Karima, his future wife, at this place. From 1607 until 1627, Malik Ambar presided over the Nizamshahi dynasty of Ahmednagar as regent, a period of significant sultanate expansion. He was instrumental in building a formidable cavalry corps and successfully fending off Jahangir's invasions. Hence, the correct answer is "Ahmednagar".

q: The famous Arabic scholar AI Biruni came in contact with India through ____ in the eleventh century AD.
subject: history
exp: The famous Arabic scholar Al-Biruni came in contact with India through Mahmud Ghaznavi in the eleventh century. Mahmud of Ghazni conducted several military campaigns in the Indian subcontinent, including the raid on the Somnath temple. Al-Biruni was a famous Persian polymath, scholar, and scientist. His work "Kitab al- Hind" provided invaluable insights into the culture, society, and scientific knowledge of India during the 11th century. Hence, the correct answer is "Mahmud Ghaznavi".

q: The art of decoration, called Pietra dura became popular during whose reign?
subject: history
exp: The art of decoration known as Pietra dura became popular during the reign of Shah Jahan. He was famously associated with the construction of the magnificent Taj Mahal. Shah Jahan's reign (1628–1658) is considered a golden era for Mughal art and architecture. Pietra dura, which translates to "hard stone" in Italian, is an intricate and highly skilled decorative technique involving the use of various colourful and finely cut gemstones to create stunning designs. The intricate and delicate Pietra dura work, which involves using precious and semi-precious stones to create ornate inlays on marble surfaces, was widely employed in this period to embellish various Mughal monuments, including the Taj Mahal and other structures within the Agra Fort. Hence the correct answer is "Shah Jahan".

q: Zafar Khan was a famous General of which of the following rulers of the Delhi Sultanate?
subject: history
exp: Zafar Khan was a military general who led Alauddin Khilji's army in several campaigns. He assisted the sultan in capturing key territories, including Multan, Sivistan, and Jaran Manjur. Zafar Khan died in the Battle of Kili against the Mongols in 1299 AD. Hence, the correct answer is "Alauddin Khilji".

q: Which of the following cities was sacked by victorious armies after the battle of Talikota?
subject: history
exp: The Battle of Talikota was a significant historical event that took place in 1565. It was a major conflict between the Deccan Sultanates and the Vijayanagara Empire. In 1565 Rama Raya, the chief minister of Vijayanagara, led the army into battle at Rakshasi-Tangadi (also known as Talikota), where his forces were routed by the combined armies of Bijapur, Ahmadnagar and Golconda. The victorious armies sacked the city of Vijayanagara. The city was totally abandoned within a few years. Hence, the correct answer is "Vijayanagar".

q: Nana Sahib, a rebel at Kanpur was the son of which of the following Peshwas?
subject: history
exp: Nana Sahib, also known as Nana Sahib Peshwa, was a prominent figure during the Indian Rebellion of 1857, which is often referred to as the Indian Mutiny or Sepoy Mutiny. He was one of the leaders of the rebellion against British colonial rule in India. Nana Sahib was not actually the biological son of any prominent historical figure; rather, he was adopted into the Peshwa family. Nana Sahib's real name was Dhondu Pant and he was the adopted son of Baji Rao II, who was the Peshwa (prime minister) of the Maratha Empire. Baji Rao II was the last Peshwa of the Maratha Empire before it was annexed by the British East India Company. After the British annexation, Nana Sahib was denied a pension and certain privileges that were promised to him by the British authorities, which is believed to have contributed to his involvement in the rebellion. Nana Sahib played a significant role in the uprising, particularly in the city of Kanpur (formerly Cawnpore). He led rebel forces in the defence of Kanpur against the British and was involved in several key events during the rebellion. The uprising ultimately led to widespread violence and conflict between Indian rebels and British forces. Hence, the correct answer is "Baji Rao II".

q: Sisodhya Rajput ruler of Mewar,Amar Singh accepted Mughal services during the reign of ______.
subject: history
exp: Amar Singh, a Sisodiya Rajput ruler of Mewar, sought Mughal support during the reign of Emperor Jahangir (1605– 1627) due to the volatile political landscape of the time. The Mughals, led by Jahangir, intervened in the internal affairs of Mewar, and Amar Singh chose to align with them to counter external threats. He entered into a treaty with Jahangir, recognizing Mughal suzerainty and agreeing to provide troops and resources in return for protection. This alliance marked a significant shift in the power dynamics of the region and had lasting consequences for Mewar's relationship with the Mughal Empire. Hence, the correct answer is "Jahangir".

q: The 'Pattanulkars' migrated from the Gujarat region to the Vijayanagar state. They Were _____.
subject: history
exp: The Pattunulkars were a community of silk weavers who migrated from the Gujarat region to the Vijayanagara Empire in the 14th century. Renowned for their expertise, vibrant designs, and innovative techniques, they revitalized the empire's silk industry. Their influence extended beyond the empire's borders, fostering silk production and design across India even after the empire's decline. Today, the Pattunulkars remain a vital community of silk weavers, contributing to India's rich weaving heritage. Hence, the correct answer is "silk weavers".

q: Who among the following was the head of the Diwan-i-Insha department under the Delhi sultanate?
subject: history
exp: The Dabir-i-Khas, also known as the Diwan-i-Khas, was a department under the Delhi Sultanate responsible for handling imperial correspondence and private consultations with the ruler. Barid-i-Mumalik: Postmaster General, in charge of the royal postal system and communication networks. Amir-i-Dad: The law enforcement official who executed the judges' orders. He heard appeals from Qazi's rulings and looked into accusations against senior officials. Wakil-i-Dar: Minister of the Household, managing royal household affairs and court functions. Hence, the correct answer is "Dabir-i- Khas".

q: By what name did Allauddin Khilji build his capital?
subject: history
exp: Allauddin Khilji, the famous medieval ruler of the Delhi Sultanate, established his new capital city called Siri. This city was founded in 1303 AD after he decided to move his capital from the older city of Delhi. The primary reason for establishing Siri was to fortify his rule and centralize his power. It was strategically located on the outskirts of Delhi to protect against possible Mongol invasions. Siri was also known for its impressive fortifications, including thick walls and bastions, making it a formidable stronghold. Hence, the correct answer is "Siri".

q: Diwan-i-Arz was essentially the department of ______ under Delhi Sultanate.
subject: history
exp: Diwan-i-Arz was essentially the department of the military or defence under the Delhi Sultanate. It was responsible for the administration and management of the military forces, including matters related to recruitment, training, provisioning, deployment, and overall defence strategy. The Diwan-i-Arz played a crucial role in maintaining the military strength and readiness of the empire, ensuring its security and defense against external threats and internal rebellions. The officials in charge of the Diwan-i-Arz were responsible for organizing the armed forces, maintaining their equipment and supplies, and coordinating military campaigns and operations on behalf of the Sultanate. Hence, the correct answer is "military services".

q: The Amara-Nayakas were _____in Vijayanagara Empire.
subject: history
exp: Known as the Nayaks of the Amara Dynasty, the Amara-Nayakas ruled South India as governors and notable military leaders. They ruled during the Vijayanagara Empire's existence in the 14th and 17th centuries. They were tasked with upholding law and order, defending the kingdom's borders, and exercising considerable influence over different parts of the empire. The military leadership of the Amara- Nayakas was essential in defending the interests of the kingdom and played a significant part in the governance and administration of the Vijayanagara Empire. Notable advancements in architecture, culture, and the arts were also witnessed in the areas under their authority. Hence, the correct answer is "military commanders".

q: During Alauddin Khilji's reign, the cloth market was known as _________.
o: More than one of the above
subject: history
exp: In Alauddin Khilji's era, the cloth market was termed "Sarai-i-Adl". This marketplace was dedicated to trading clothes and luxury items. It formed an essential part of the economic structure, facilitating the sale of high-quality goods. The market's significance lies in its role as a hub for cloth trade and upscale products. Through this, Khilji's administration aimed to regulate commerce and ensure quality. This historical insight showcases the Sultan's economic strategies and his emphasis on organized trade, highlighting the cultural and economic complexities of the time. Hence, the correct answer is "Sarai-i-Adl".

q: Imadshahi family was known for the foundation of which state?
subject: history
exp: The Imadshahi family is primarily associated with the foundation of the state of Berar. The Imad Shahi dynasty, also known as the Imadshahi rulers, held significant influence in the Berar region during the medieval period. The Imad Shahi family originated from Persia and established their rule in the Indian Deccan during the 15th and 16th centuries. They established their rule in the Deccan region of India, with Berar as one of their prominent territories. Berar, located in what is now the central part of India, witnessed the presence and rule of the Imad Shahi dynasty. Hence the correct answer is "Berar".

q: He was a Delhi Sultan who started the practice of Sijada and Paibos in the court. Who was he?
subject: history
exp: Ghiyasuddin Balban, a Delhi Sultan, is known for starting the practice of Sijada and Paibos in the court. These were acts of showing extreme respect and submission to the ruler. Ghiyas ud din Balban, was a powerful ruler who controlled the Delhi Sultanate from 1266 to 1286. He is also known for the policy of 'blood and iron.' Hence The correct answer is Ghiyasuddin Balban.

q: Who had assumed the title of Balban?
subject: history
exp: The title of "Balban" was assumed by Ulugh Khan, who later became the Sultan of Delhi and is historically known as Sultan Ghiyas-ud-Din Balban. Kabir Khan, Farid Khan, and Ayaz Khan were not associated with this title. Balban is famous for his reign in the Delhi Sultanate, known for his strong and centralized rule and efforts to assert royal authority. Hence, the correct answer is "Ulugh Khan".

q: Vijayanagara emperor Krishnadeva Raya founded a suburban township near Vijayanagara called Nagalapuram after his:
subject: history
exp: Emperor Krishnadeva Raya's decision to establish a suburban township and name it Nagalapuram after his mother demonstrates the deep emotional connection he held with his family. Krishnadeva Raya, who reigned from 1509 to 1529 CE, is often celebrated as a remarkable monarch known for his military prowess, administrative skills, patronage of arts and literature, and fostering a golden age for the Vijayanagara Empire. The Vijayanagara Empire was a prominent and powerful South Indian empire that flourished during the medieval period. The Vijayanagara Empire was founded in 1336 CE by Harihara I and his brother Bukka Raya, who were military commanders under the Kakatiya dynasty. Hence, the correct answer is “mother”.

q: Who built the temple city Osain, near Jodhpur?
subject: history
exp: The temple city Osain, near Jodhpur, was built by Gurjara Pratihara King Vatsaraja. The Osian Temple is located 65 km north of Jodhpur. It is in the Thar desert, on the Jodhpur- Bikaner highway. It is also known as the Khajuraho of Rajasthan. Hence, the correct answer is "Pratiharas".

q: The first Europeans to come to India were
subject: history
exp: The Portuguese were the first Europeans to arrive in India and the last to leave. In c. 1498 CE, Vasco da Gama of Portugal discovered a new sea route from Europe to India. In 1453, the Ottoman Turks captured Constantinople and this brought the regular trade routes under the control of the Turks. This urged the Europeans to search for new trade routes, especially by sea. Hence, the correct answer is "Portuguese".

q: Do or Die' is associated with which of the movements in India's freedom struggle ?
subject: history
exp: The slogan "Do or Die" is associated with the Quit India Movement launched by Mahatma Gandhi in 1942. Here's a more detailed explanation: Context: During World War II, Mahatma Gandhi launched the Quit India Movement (also known as the August Movement) demanding an end to British rule in India. Slogan: In his Quit India speech on August 8, 1942, at the Gowalia Tank Maidan in Bombay, Gandhi urged people to "Do or Die". Significance: The slogan "Do or Die" reflected the Indian people's determination to achieve independence from British rule at any cost. Other Movements: Dandi March: This was a civil disobedience movement led by Gandhi to protest the British salt monopoly. Non-Cooperation Movement: This was a widespread civil disobedience campaign launched by Gandhi in 1920 to boycott British institutions and products. Khilafat Movement: This movement protested against the British government's actions regarding the Ottoman Empire. Hence, the correct answer is option (D).

q: In 1918, Mahatma Gandhi organised a satyagraha to support the peasants of the Kheda district of ______.
subject: history
exp: Mahatma Gandhi started satyagraha movements in various places after arriving in India. Gandhiji started Satyagraha in the Kheda district of Gujarat in support of the peasants. The Champaran Satyagraha of 1917 was the first satyagraha movement led by Mahatma Gandhi. The following year, 1918, Gandhiji was involved in two campaigns in his home state of Gujarat. First, Gandhi JI arose in a labor dispute in Ahmedabad, demanding better working conditions for the textile mill workers. After that, he joined peasants in Kheda in demanding the state for the reduction of taxes following the failure of their harvest. Hence, the correct option is “Gujarat”.

q: The queen with the title Didda ruled over which part of India between 980-1003?
subject: history
exp: Didda was the daughter of Simharaja who was king of Lohara. Didda ruled over Kashmir from 958 CE to 1003 CE. She was married to the King of Kashmir Ksemagupta to unite the Lohara with her Kingdom of her husband. She first acted as regent for her son and became the sole monarch from 980 CE. Source of information about Didda is Rajtarangini written by Kalhana. Hence, the correct answer is “Kashmir”.

q: In which year (in AD) was the East India Company established?
subject: history
exp: The English East India Company, formally known as “The Governor and Company of Merchants of London Trading into the East Indies”, was established in 1600 AD. In 1609 AD, William Hawkins visited the court of Jahangir to seek permission to establish a factory at Surat, but he was not successful. In 1613 AD, the English established their first factory at Surat with the permission of the Mughal authorities. In 1615 AD, Sir Thomas Roe visited Jahangir’s court as an ambassador of James I and secured trade privileges for the English in India. Hence, the correct answer is “1600”.

q: Arrest of _________ in 1930 led to angry demonstrations in the streets of Peshawar.
subject: history
exp: Arrest of Abdul Ghaffar Khan in 1930 led to angry demonstrations on the streets of Peshawar. Khan Abdul Ghaffar Khan led the Khudai Khitmadgar which was a group of Pashtun people committed to removing British rule through non-violence. On 23 April 1930, Ghaffar Khan was arrested for giving a speech in Utmanzai urging resistance to British rule. Abul Kalam Azad was the first Education Minister of India and was awarded Bharat Ratna in 1992 posthumously. Zakir Hussain was the first Muslim President of India and was the third President of India. Hence, the correct answer is Abdul Ghaffar Khan. Muzaffar Ahmed was a politician and was one of the founders of the communist movement in India. Hence, the correct answer is “Abdul Ghaffar Khan”.

q: The Poona Pact was signed between Mahatma Gandhi and _____.
subject: history
exp: The Poona Pact was an agreement signed on 24th September 1932. It was an understanding reached between Mahatma Gandhi and Dr. B. R. Ambedkar. The pact focused on the reservation of electoral seats for the depressed classes in British India's legislative assemblies. The signing took place at Yerwada Central Jail in Poona, where Gandhi was imprisoned at the time. The agreement aimed to ensure fair representation and upliftment of the depressed classes within the political framework. Hence, the correct answer is "B. R. Ambedkar."

q: Lord Cornwallis is known for ____.
o: land revenue settlement of United States
subject: history
exp: Lord Cornwallis took charge as the Commander-in-chief of British India and The Governor- General of Fort William Bengal. He served as the Governor-General of India from 1786 to 1793. He introduced the Permanent settlement system in Bengal and other parts of the country. Warren Hastings attacked the caste system. Sir Thomas Munro introduced the ryotwari settlement in Madras. Principle of Ryotwari Settlement: Direct collection of the land revenue by the government from each individual cultivator termed as "ryots".Hence removing the middlemen. Hence, the correct answer is “permanent revenue settlement of Bengal”.

q: During their rule the British persuaded or forced cultivators in Assam to grow
subject: history
exp: During their rule, the British forced cultivators in various parts of India to produce crops that could bring economic benefits to their trade in India. They forced farmers to produce tea in Assam wheat in Punjab sugarcane in Uttar Pradesh, cotton in Maharashtra jute in Bengal rice in Madras Though they were commercially benefitted from the same but it had a negative impact on the agricultural land and to the lives of peasants and farmers working on those lands due to very less return.It was also one of the reasons for nationalist movements in India. Hence, the correct answer is “Tea”.

q: Who gave the slogan 'Back to the Vedas (Vedo ki aur lauto)'?
subject: history
exp: Swami Dayanand Saraswati gave the slogan ‘ Back to Vedas.’ Dayanand Saraswati wanted to put society in touch with its roots so going back to vedas was the best way. Arya Samaj was founded by Swami Dayananda Saraswati in 1875. He translated the Vedas and wrote three books named Satyartha Prakash, Veda Bhasya & Veda Bhasya Bhumika. Dayanand Anglo Vedic schools were established based on his philosophy. Mission Leader Founder of Sikhism Guru Nanak Ji Ramkrishna Mission Swami Vivekananda Brahmo Samaj Raja Ram Mohan Roy Hence the correct answer is “ Dayanand Saraswati.”

q: Who was the founder of Banaras Hindu University?
subject: history
exp: Banaras Hindu University was established by the ‘B.H.U. Act 1915’ in 1916. Madan Mohan Malaviya served as the Vice-Chancellor of BHU from 1919 to 1938. He was a prominent Indian educationist and politician. Malaviya played a significant role in the Indian independence movement. His vision for BHU was to blend traditional Indian education with modern Western learning. Under his leadership, BHU became one of the premier educational institutions in India. Hence, the correct answer is "Madan Mohan Malaviya."

q: Who was the pioneer of the Chipko movement of 1973?
subject: history
exp: The Chipko Movement was started by Sunderlal Bahuguna and the movement picked up pace in 1973. The movement was begun to prevent the cutting of trees. The Chipko Movement followed the Gandhian method of nonviolence and Satyagraha. Sambhaji Bhosale was the eldest son of Shivaji, the founder of the Maratha Empire. He was the second Chhatrapati of the Maratha Empire. He ruled from 1681 to 1689. Baba Amte was an Indian social worker and activist known mainly for his work in rehabilitating and empowering people suffering from leprosy. Medha Patkar has been a central organizer and strategist for Narmada Bachao Andolan (NBA), a people's movement organized to stop the construction of a series of dams planned for India's largest westward-flowing river, the Narmada. Hence, the correct answer is “Sunderlal Bahuguna”.

q: Which one is the correct chronological order of the following events? I. Quit India Movement II. Shimla Conference III. Poona Pact IV. Cabinet Mission
subject: history
exp: Quit India movement is also known as August Kranti movement and was launched on 8th August 1942 by Mahatma Gandhi demanding an end of British Rule in the country. Shimla Conference was held in 1945 and was convened to agree on Wavell's plan for self Governance for India and to provide separate representation to communal lines. Poona pact was held on 24th September 1932 between Mahatma Gandhi and B.R. Ambedkar who accepted that the depressed class is the most discriminated section of Indian society. Cabinet Mission came in 1946 to discuss the transfer of power from the British government to Indian leaders. Hence, the correct answer is “III, I, II, IV”.

q: Name the British General who was responsible for the Jallianwalla Bagh massacre.
subject: history
exp: The Jallianwala Bagh massacre occurred on April 13, 1919, during the British colonial rule in India. Troops of the British Indian Army opened fire on unarmed civilians who had gathered in Jallianwala Bagh in Amritsar. The orders to open fire were given by Colonel Reginald Edward Harry Dyer. The incident resulted in a significant loss of life and sparked outrage across India. Dyer's actions were widely condemned, both in India and in Britain, leading to a formal inquiry known as the Hunter Commission. The massacre played a crucial role in shaping the Indian independence movement. Hence, the correct answer is "Dyer."

q: During their rule, the British persuaded or forced cultivators in Madras to grow _________.
subject: history
exp: The British monarch decided that they will use Indian villages to grow crops like wheat, rice, jute, and Tea. These crops were in demand in Britain. Britishres forces Indian farmers to grow Jute in Bengal, Tea in Assam, Sugarcane in Uttar Pradesh, cotton in Maharashtra, and Rice in Madras. Hence, the correct answer is “Rice”.

q: Who was the founder of the Ghadar Party?
subject: history
exp: The Ghadar Party was formed in 1913 in the United States and initially, it was known as the Pacific Coast Hindustan Association. The Ghadar Movement was an international political movement started by deported Indians to overthrow British rule in India. It was founded under the leadership of Lala Har Dayal, Sant Baba Wasakha Singh Dadehar, Baba Jawala Singh, Santokh Singh and Sohan Singh Bhakna as its president. Headquarter - San Francisco In 1914, after the outbreak of World War I, many members of the Ghadar party returned to Punjab to incite an armed revolution for Indian Independence. The Ghadar party was formally dissolved in 1948. Hence, the correct answer is “Lala Hardayal”.

q: Murshid Quli Khan Alivardi Khan and Sirajuddaullah were all nawabs of ___________
subject: history
exp: Murshid Quli Khan, Alivaridi Khan, and Sirajuddaullah were nawabs of Bengal. Murshid Quli Khan was the first nawab of Bengal. Alivardi Khan was a nawab of Bengal from 1740-1756. Sirajuddaullah was the last independent Nawab of Bengal. The end of his reign marked the beginning of British East India Company rule over Bengal. Hence, the correct answer is "Bengal".

q: Which Governor General abolished the 'Sati System' in India?
subject: history
exp: Lord William Bentinck was the Governor General of India who took significant steps to abolish the Sati system. He issued Regulation XVII in December 1829, which declared the practice of Sati illegal. Sati, the practice where a widow was compelled to self-immolate on her husband's funeral pyre, was prevalent in some parts of India. The regulation not only banned the practice but also made it punishable by law. Bentinck's decision was influenced by both humanitarian considerations and pressures from reformist groups. Hence, the correct answer is "Lord William Bentinck."

q: Who was the first Viceroy of India?
subject: history
exp: Under the Government of India Act of 1858, the power was transferred from the East India Company to the British Crown. The Governor General was given the title of Viceroy. Lord Canning was the first Viceroy of India. His tenure was from 1858-1862. Lord Mountbatten was the last Viceroy of India. Lord Curzon is associated with the Partition of Bengal. Lord Wavell was the second last Viceroy and was succeeded by Lord Mountbatten. Hence, the correct answer is “Lord Canning”.

q: Which of the following was a leader of the Hindustan Socialist Republican Army founded in 1928?
subject: history
exp: Hindustan Socialist Republican Army was established in 1928 at Feroz Shah Kotla in New Delhi. Leaders associated with this army were Sukhdev Thapar, Bhagat Singh, Chandrashekhar Azad, etc. Its main leader was Chandrashekhar Azad. This party was initially named as Hindustan Republican Association which was formed by Ram Prasad Bismil in 1923 at Allahabad. Most famous incident by HSRA was the Kakori conspiracy which occurred on 9th August 1925 in which party members looted a train carrying government money near Lucknow. Hence, the correct answer is “Chandra Shekhar Azad”.

q: Who raised the slogan "Swaraj is my birthright and I shall have it"?
subject: history
exp: Bal Gangadhar Tilak gave the popular slogan, "Swaraj is my birthright and I shall have it" in the Bombay State Political Conference that was held in 1916. Bal Gangadhar Tilak was popularly known as Lokmanya Tilak. He formed a prominent organization named Deccan Education Society that promotes modern learning. He established Fergusson College at Pune . He, along with Annie Besant, played a remarkable role during the Home Rule Movement (1916-18). Mahratta & Kesari were the two newspapers started by Bal Gangadhar Tilak. Hence, the correct answer is "Bal Gangadhar Tilak".

q: Gol Gumbaz was designed by?
subject: history
exp: The Gol Gumbaz, also called Gol Gumbadh ,was constructed by Adil Shah II. It was designed by an architect Yaqut of Dabul. It is located in Bijapur and in Bijapur it is known as the "Whispering Gallery". The architectural style of Gumbaz is Deccan Indo- Islamic. Hence, the correct answer is “Yaqut of Dabul”.

q: Who was the first Governor General of Bengal?
subject: history
exp: The first governor-general in India (of Bengal) was Warren Hastings in 1773. He began his career as a writer (clerk) in the East India Company at Calcutta in 1750. Warren Hastings established the revenue board in 1772, aiming for better financial administration. He abolished the dual system of governance, streamlining the administrative structure. In partnership with William Jones, he founded the Asiatic Society of Bengal in 1784 in Calcutta, promoting learning and research. Hastings laid the groundwork for the civil service, which was later reformed and modernized by Lord Cornwallis. Hence, the correct answer is "Warren Hastings".

q: Which act ended the "Trade Monopoly" of the East India Company?
subject: history
exp: The Regulating Act of 1773 officially, the East India Company Act 1772 was an Act of the Parliament of Great Britain that proposed to refurbish the management of the East India Company's rule in India. But the Act did not prove to be a long-term solution to concerns over the company's affairs. So Pitt's India Act was therefore subsequently passed in 1784 as a more radical reform. The East India Company's monopoly over trade with China and tea with India was protected by the Charter Act of 1813, which also eliminated the company's monopoly on trade in India. The East India Company's business was given an additional 20 years of rule. The act gave permission to those who liked to visit India to expand their moral and religious beliefs. It monitored the company's commercial benefits and territorial revenues. It was hoped that it would keep its commercial and territorial accounts independent. The Charter Act of 1833 also called the Saint Helena Act of 1833 or the Government of India Act of 1833. This act was the first act in which natives of India were free to participate in a democratic system of government. Hence, the correct answer is “The Charter Act of 1813”.

q: Who led the Bardoli Satyagraha movement?
subject: history
exp: Bardoli Satyagraha occurred in 1928 in Gujarat during the British Raj. Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel led the movement. The movement aimed to reduce taxes due to the year's calamities. Patel instructed Bardoli farmers to refuse tax payment and remain non-violent. The struggle sought not only tax cancellation but also the return of seized property. Hence, the correct answer is "Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel."

q: In 1918, Mahatma Gandhi went to _________ organise a satyagraha movement amongst cotton mill workers.
subject: history
exp: Mahatma Gandhi organized the Satyagraha movement amongst cotton mill workers at Ahmedabad in 1918. Cotton mill workers were demanding a 50% wage hike but mill owners were willing to give a hike of only 20%. Gandhi went on a hunger strike to support mill workers. As a result, a hike of 35% was given to workers. Champararan Satyagraha of 1917 was the first satyagraha led by Mahatma Gandhi. Kheda Satyagraha of 1918 was the third satyagraha of Gandhiji. Hence, the correct answer is "Ahmedabad."

q: Where was the 'Azad Hind Fauj' founded?
subject: history
exp: The Azad Hind Fauj, also known as the Indian National Army (INA), was founded in 1942 in Singapore by Mohan Singh. The Azad Hind Fauj was revived by Subhas Chandra Bose in 1943, during World War II with the goal of liberating India from British colonial rule. The INA's initial goal was to recruit Indian soldiers who were prisoners of war or had been captured by the Japanese, but it later expanded to include Indian civilians living in Southeast Asia. Hence, the correct answer is “Singapore”.

q: Gol Gumbaz is located in__________.
subject: history
exp: Gol Gumbaz is located in Jadar Galli, Vijayapura, Karnataka. Mohammed Adil Shah (ruled 1627-1657) built the Gol Gumbaz which is the fourth- largest dome in the world and the largest in India. A particular attraction of the Gol Gambaz is the central chamber, where every sound is echoed seven times. Gol Gumbaz took 29 years to complete and is almost double the size of the Taj Mahal with massive measurements. Hence, the correct answer is “Karnataka”.

q: In 1917, which movement was launched by Mahatma Gandhi from Champaran?
subject: history
exp: Champaran Satyagraha was the first Satyagraha movement by Mahatma Gandhi in India. It is considered an important revolt in the Indian Freedom Struggle. It was a farmer uprising that took place in Bihar. Farmers were opposing the forced cultivation of indigo. This movement was started in 1917. Non-Cooperation Movement was conducted in 1920-1922 after the Jallianwala Bagh Massacre. The Quit India movement started on 8 August 1942 by Mahatma Gandhi. The Swadeshi Movement was started on 7th August 1905 to boycott British products. Hence, the correct answer is “Satyagraha”.

q: In which year was the Battle of Saragarhi fought?
subject: history
exp: The year 1897 witnessed the historic Battle of Saragarhi, an epic conflict that took place between British Indian Army soldiers and Afghan tribesmen. It was a significant battle between the British Indian Army and Afghan tribesmen during the Tirah Campaign in the North-West Frontier Province (now Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Pakistan). The battle took place on September 12, 1897, at the Saragarhi outpost, where a small contingent of 21 soldiers from the 36th Sikhs (now the 4th Battalion of the Sikh Regiment) valiantly defended the post against a much larger force of several thousand Afghan tribesmen. The soldiers fought with immense bravery and sacrifice, making it one of the most remarkable last stands in military history. Hence, the correct answer is "1897".

q: In which year was the 'Battle of Goa' fought?
subject: history
exp: The Battle of Goa was fought in the year 1510 between the Portuguese Armada and the Dutch East India Company fleets attempting to blockade and conquer the city of Goa. Afonso de Albuquerque also called Afonso de Albuquerque the Great succeeded Dom Francisco de Almeida as Governor of the Portuguese State of India on November 4, 1509. The Marshal of Portugal Dom Fernando Coutinho who was sent by King Manuel had been ordered to enforce the orderly succession of Albuquerque as Governor. In 1510, the governor of Portuguese India Afonso de Albuquerque captured the city of Goa and Melaka in the year 1511 on the Malay Peninsula. Hence, the correct answer is "1510".

q: The _____ was a group of seven Members of Parliament from the United Kingdom, constituted to suggest constitutional reforms for British India.
subject: history
exp: The Simon Commission, officially known as the Indian Statutory Commission, was formed in 1927 by the British government to propose constitutional reforms for British India. Sir John Simon, a British politician, led it. The commission consisted of seven members, all of whom were Members of Parliament from the United Kingdom. The purpose of the commission was to assess the progress of constitutional reforms and to recommend changes in governance in India. However, the commission faced strong opposition from Indian political parties and organizations, who criticized the absence of Indian representation. This led to widespread protests and boycotts, marking an important milestone in the Indian independence movement. Hence, the correct answer is "Simon Commission".

q: When did Tipu Sultan die at war with the British?
subject: history
exp: Tipu Sultan died at war with the British on May 4, 1799. He was the ruler of the Kingdom of Mysore in South India and had been engaged in a long-running conflict with the British East India Company over control of the region. In 1799, a British-led coalition launched a campaign against Tipu Sultan's forces, and after a series of battles, they captured the city of Seringapatam, which was the capital of Mysore. Hence, the correct answer is “1799”.

q: Who was the Indian Army Chief at the time of Bangladesh Liberation War?
subject: history
exp: Sam Hormusji Framji Jamshedji Manekshaw, also known as Sam Manekshaw, was a distinguished military officer and the first Indian Army officer to be promoted to the rank of Field Marshal. Manekshaw joined the British Indian Army in 1932 and served with distinction during World War II and the Indo- Pakistani War of 1971. He played a crucial role in India's victory in the 1971 war against Pakistan, which led to the creation of Bangladesh. Hence, the correct answer is "Sam Manekshaw".

q: In which year did Vasco De Gama land in Calicut (Kozikode)?
subject: history
exp: Vasco De Gama landed in Calicut (Kozhikode) on 20th May 1498 and left Calicut on 29 August 1498. He was the first Portuguese sailor who reached India and the person who discovered the sea route to India from Europe. He sailed from Lisbon in Portugal via the Cape of Good Hope and reached Calicut. Hence, the correct answer is "1498".

q: The Indian National Association was established in 1876 by in Calcutta.
subject: history
exp: The Indian National Association (INA) was a political organization founded by Surendranath Banerjee and Ananda Mohan Bose in 1876. It was one of the earliest Indian political organizations to demand self- government and was formed as a response to the ill-treatment of Indian subjects by the British colonial government. The INA was active in Kolkata, then the capital of British India, and it held meetings and published a newspaper called "The Indian Nation". The INA was instrumental in organizing the first session of the Indian National Congress in 1885 and many of its members went on to become prominent leaders of the Indian independence movement. The organization was dissolved in 1887, but its legacy lived on in the Indian nationalist movement. Hence, the correct answer is "Ananda Mohan Bose".

q: When was the 'Battle of Tukaroi' fought?
subject: history
exp: The 'Battle of Tukaroi' was fought in the year 1575. It was a significant conflict that took place in the Indian subcontinent during the reign of Emperor Akbar. The battle was fought between the Mughal Empire, led by Akbar, and the combined forces of the Afghan tribes led by Daud Khan. The Afghan tribes were attempting to challenge Mughal authority in the region. The battle resulted in a decisive victory for the Mughals, consolidating their control over the region. The victory at the Battle of Tukaroi further solidified Akbar's rule and marked a turning point in the Mughal Empire's expansion in the Indian subcontinent. Hence, the correct answer is "1575".

q: The Aligarh Movement was started by___.
subject: history
exp: Syed Ahmed Khan took the initiative to initiate the Aligarh Movement. Syed Ahmed Khan, a notable figure in 19th-century India, played significant roles as a Muslim reformer, philosopher, and educationalist. He believed in the importance of modern education and social reforms for the upliftment of the Muslim community. In 1875, Syed Ahmed Khan founded the Muhammadan Anglo-Oriental College in Aligarh, Uttar Pradesh (now Aligarh Muslim University), which became the center of the Aligarh Movement. The movement aimed to promote education, social reforms, and the empowerment of Muslims through modern knowledge and skills. Syed Ahmed Khan's efforts played a significant role in the development of a modern Muslim identity in India. Hence, the correct answer is "Syed Ahmed Khan".

q: The East India Company sent Captain William Hawkins to the court of Emperor______ in 1608 to seek permission to open a factory at Surat.
subject: history
exp: In 1608, the East India Company dispatched Captain William Hawkins to the court of Emperor Jahangir, the fourth Mughal emperor of India, to seek permission to establish a factory in Surat. The mission marked an important milestone in the early British interactions with the Mughal Empire. Captain Hawkins presented gifts and letters from King James I of England to Jahangir, attempting to secure trade privileges. While the negotiations were not entirely successful in obtaining favorable terms, they established a diplomatic and commercial connection between the British and the Mughal Empire, laying the groundwork for future British presence and trade in India. Hence, the correct answer is "Jahangir".

q: The Victoria Memorial, conceived by Lord Curzon, represents the architectural climax of _________ city.
subject: history
exp: The Victoria Memorial is indeed an iconic landmark in Kolkata (formerly Calcutta), India, and it was conceived during the tenure of Lord Curzon as the Viceroy and Governor-General of India. It was built between 1906 and 1921 as a tribute to Queen Victoria. British architect Sir William Emerson designed the memorial in the Indo- Saracenic architectural style, which combines elements of British and Mughal architecture. Lord Curzon, whose full name was George Nathaniel Curzon, was a British statesman and colonial administrator who served as the Viceroy and Governor- General of India from 1899 to 1905. He implemented administrative reforms, including the division of Bengal into East Bengal and West Bengal. Hence, the correct answer is “Kolkata”.

q: _____ is well-known for the golden beautification of the Harmandir Sahib Gurdwara in Amritsar, famously known as the Golden Temple.
subject: history
exp: Ranjit Singh, the legendary Sikh ruler, is renowned for the golden beautification of the Harmandir Sahib Gurdwara in Amritsar, commonly known as the Golden Temple. During his reign in the early 19th century, Ranjit Singh ordered the gurdwara's upper floors and domes to be covered in gold leaf, giving it a striking appearance. This act of opulent adornment was aimed at enhancing the spiritual significance and grandeur of the sacred site. The Golden Temple stands as a symbol of Sikhism and serves as a significant pilgrimage destination for Sikhs around the world, attracting millions of visitors who are mesmerized by its majestic golden exterior. Hence, the correct answer is "Ranjit Singh".

q: On March 23rd, India observes _____-day/divas as a tribute to Bhagat Singh, Sukhdev and Rajguru on their death anniversary.
subject: history
exp: Bhagat Singh, Sukhdev, and Rajguru were revolutionary freedom fighters who played a significant role in India's struggle for independence from British colonial rule. On March 23, 1931, Bhagat Singh, Sukhdev, and Rajguru were hanged in Lahore Central Jail for their involvement in the murder of John Saunders, a British police officer. Their execution had a profound impact on the Indian independence movement and inspired many to continue the struggle for freedom. Bhagat Singh was in the village of Banga in the Punjab region. He was a member of the Hindustan Socialist Republican Association (HSRA). Sukhdev Thapar was born in Ludhiana, Punjab. he was a member of the HSRA and played a key role in the Lahore Conspiracy Case. Shivaram Rajguru was born in the village of Khed in Maharashtra. He was also a member of the HSRA and was involved in several revolutionary activities in the country. Bhagat Singh, Sukhdev, and Rajguru are remembered as national heroes in India and are considered martyrs for their sacrifice in the fight for India's independence. Hence, the correct answer is “Shaheed Divas”.

q: The movement of the local farmers of Bardoli in Gujarat against the British in 1928 was led by______
subject: history
exp: Vallabhbhai Patel was the leader of the local farmers' movement in Bardoli, Gujarat, which emerged as a powerful resistance against the British in 1928. The Bardoli Satyagraha was a significant nonviolent protest organized by Vallabhbhai Patel and his colleagues in response to the oppressive policies and high land taxes imposed by the British Raj. Vallabhbhai Patel, who later became the first Deputy Prime Minister and the first Minister of Home Affairs of independent India, played a crucial role in mobilizing the farmers and leading the successful resistance against the British authorities. His leadership during the Bardoli Satyagraha earned him the title of "Sardar" or "The Iron Man of India." Hence, the correct answer is "Vallabhbhai Patel".

q: Which one of the following was NOT a condition laid down in the Gandhi-Irwin Pact?
o: The Indian National Congress should stop the civil disobedience movement
Removal of salt tax; allowing to produce, trade and sell legally
Withdrawal of tax on khadi production
Participation in the Round Table Conference by the Indian National Congress
subject: history
exp: The Gandhi-Irwin Pact, also known as the Delhi Pact, was signed between Mahatma Gandhi and Lord Irwin, the Viceroy of India, on 5th March 1931. The pact aimed to end the Civil Disobedience Movement and promote dialogue between the Indian National Congress and the British government. It included several conditions, such as the Indian National Congress agreeing to stop the civil disobedience movement, participation in the Round Table Conference, and the release of political prisoners. However, the withdrawal of tax on khadi production was not specifically mentioned as a condition in the pact. The pact marked an important step in the Indian freedom struggle and paved the way for further negotiations. Hence, the correct answer is "Withdrawal of tax on khadi production".

q: Mitra Mela was a revolutionary organisation founded by Veer Savarkar in the year___.
subject: history
exp: Mitra Mela was a revolutionary organization founded by Veer Savarkar in the year 1899. Veer Savarkar was an Indian independence activist and a prominent figure in the Indian nationalist movement. Mitra Mela aimed to promote patriotism, national unity, and social reform among Indian youth. It played a significant role in mobilizing and inspiring young revolutionaries for the cause of Indian independence. The organization emphasized the need for self-defense and self-reliance, and its members actively participated in various nationalist activities during the British colonial rule in India. Hence, the correct answer is "1899".

q: In which year was the East India Company incorporated for the exploitation of trade with East and Southeast Asia and India?
subject: history
exp: In 1600, the East India Company acquired a charter from the ruler of England, Queen Elizabeth I ,granting it the sole right to trade with the East. This meant that no other trading group in England could compete with the East India Company. With this charter, the Company could venture across the oceans, looking for new lands from which it could buy goods at a cheap price, and carry them back to Europe to sell at higher prices. Mercantile trading companies in those days made profit primarily by excluding competition, so that they could buy cheap and sell dear. Hence, the correct answer is "1600".

q: The First Anglo-Burmese War ended with the signing of the ______.
subject: history
exp: The first Anglo-Burmese War was fought between 5 March 1824 to 24 February 1826. Britain won this war against the Burmese empire. Briths won the war after the death of Burmese General Bandaula in the Battle of Ava. This war ended with 'The Treaty of Yandabo.' It was the beginning of British rule in Burma. A total of three wars were fought between the Britishers and the Burmese Empire. Hence, the correct answer is"Treaty of Yandabo."

q: Surendranath Banerjee and Ananda Mohan Bose founded____ in Bengal in 1876.
subject: history
exp: Surendranath Banerjee and Ananda Mohan Bose founded the Indian Association in Bengal in 1876. The Indian Association was a political organization aimed at addressing the social, political, and economic issues faced by the Indian people under British rule. It played a significant role in the Indian nationalist movement and worked towards promoting national consciousness and advocating for political reforms. The association provided a platform for the Indian intelligentsia to discuss and address pressing issues, and it played a crucial role in mobilizing public opinion and fostering unity among Indians in their struggle for freedom and self- governance. Hence, the correct answer is "Indian Association".

q: Opium wars were fought between the British and____.
subject: history
exp: The Opium Wars were a series of conflicts between the British Empire and China in the 19th century. The wars were primarily triggered by disputes over trade, specifically the British importation of opium into China. The British desired to maintain their lucrative opium trade, while the Chinese government sought to prohibit it due to its detrimental effects on society. The First Opium War took place from 1839 to 1842, resulting in British victory and the Treaty of Nanjing. The Second Opium War occurred from 1856 to 1860, concluding with the Treaty of Tientsin. These wars had significant consequences for China, including territorial losses, economic impositions, and a weakened central government. Hence, the correct answer is "China".

q: As a reaction to Rowlatt Act, _________ was organised as National Humiliation Day.
subject: history
exp: National Humiliation Day was organized on 6th April 1919 as a reaction to Rowlatt Act. Rowlatt Act was also known as the 'Anarchical and Revolutionary Act, of 1919.' This act was passed in March 1919 by Imperial Legislative Council. It gave the authority to Britishers to detain anybody suspected of terrorist activities without any type of trial. Rowlatt Act was also termed the 'Black Act.' Hence, the correct answer is "6th April 1919."

q: The Hindustan Socialist Republican Association (HSRA) was formed in the year _____ with an aim to overthrow the British.
subject: history
exp: HSRA was founded in 1928. It was a revolutionary organization. It was previously known as Hindustan Republican Army and Hindustan Republican Association. The name was changed due to the influence of Bhagat Singh. It was founded by Ram Prasad Bismil, Sachindranath Bakshi, Ashfaqulla Khan, and Sachindranath Sanyal. Hence, the correct answer is "1928".

q: The Khilafat Movement of 1920 was organised as a protest against the injustice done to ______.
subject: history
exp: The Khilafat movement was started in 1919. It was launched by Shaukat Ali and Muhammad Ali with Abdul Kalam Azad. It was organized to protest against the injustice done to Turkey. Indian Muslims organized this movement to recover the Capilh of Turkey. The caliph of Turkey was a symbol of the unity of Muslims in India. It was supported by Mahatma Gandhi through his Non-Copperation movement. Hence, the correct answer is "Turkey."

q: Mahatma Gandhi formed the Natal Indian Congress in the year ______.
subject: history
exp: Mahatma Gandhi formed the Natal Indian Congress in the year 1894. It was an organization formed to fight against discrimination by Britishers towards the Indians in South Africa. Mahatma Gandhi was the first Honorary Secretary of the Natal Indian Congress. Abdoola Hajee Adam Jhaveri was the inaugural President of the Natal Indian Congress. Mahatma Gandhi is also known as the 'Father of the Nation.' Hence, the correct answer is "1894."

q: Sir Thomas Roe visited the court of Mughal ruler____as the ambassador of the King of England.
subject: history
exp: As the ambassador of the King of England, Sir Thomas Roe journeyed to the court of Jahangir, the Mughal ruler, to establish diplomatic relations. Sir Thomas Roe was an English diplomat and statesman who was sent by King James I of England to establish diplomatic relations with the Mughal Empire. He arrived in the Mughal court in 1615 and spent several years building relations with Jahangir and his courtiers. His mission aimed to secure trading privileges and promote English interests in India. Roe's visit marked an important chapter in the history of Anglo-Indian relations and laid the foundation for future diplomatic and trade ties between England and the Mughal Empire. Hence, the correct answer is "Jahangir".

q: Tipu Sultan and the British East India Company signed the Treaty of Mangalore in the year___.
subject: history
exp: The Treaty of Mangalore was signed between Tipu Sultan, the ruler of the Kingdom of Mysore, and the British East India Company in the year 1784. The treaty was a result of the Second Anglo-Mysore War (1780-1784) between the two parties. The treaty brought an end to the hostilities and established peace between them. As per the terms of the treaty, both sides agreed to return the territories and prisoners of war that they had captured during the conflict. The treaty also outlined trade provisions and established diplomatic relations between the British and Tipu Sultan's kingdom. Hence, the correct answer is "1784".

q: The Sepoy Mutiny in India started from ______.
subject: history
exp: The Sepoy Mutiny was an unsuccessful rebellion during 1857–1858 against the British It started in India from Meerut by Indian troops. Sepoy Mutiny, also called the First War of Independence, spread to Lucknow, Delhi, Kanpur, and Agra. The revolt of 1857 was the first expression of organised resistance against the British East India Company. Hence, the correct answer is "Meerut".

q: British East India Company defeated the Portuguese in the____.
subject: history
exp: The British East India Company did not defeat the Portuguese in the Battle of Swally. The correct answer is the Battle of Swally, also known as the Battle of Suvali, which took place in 1612. It was a naval conflict between the British East India Company and the Portuguese navy near the town of Suvali in Gujarat, India. The British East India Company emerged victorious in this battle, marking a significant turning point in their naval power in the region and establishing their presence in India. The defeat weakened Portuguese influence and opened doors for British expansion in India. Hence, the correct answer is "Battle of Swally".

q: In which year did the Patharughat peasant revolt take place against the tax policies of the British in Assam?
subject: history
exp: In the year 1894, the Patharughat peasant revolt took place against the tax policies of the British in the Darrang district of Assam. On 28 January 1894, around 140 peasants were killed in unprovoked police firing while protesting revenue hikes. Many people were injured and due to this reason, Patharu-ghat is known as Assam’s Jallianwala Bagh which is in Punjab. Hence, the correct answer is "1894".

q: The Bangladesh Liberation War ended on _____.
subject: history
exp: Liberation War of Bangladesh ended on 16th December 1971. It led to the independence of Bangladesh. Before Independence, Bangladesh was also known as "East Pakistan." War was started when Pakistan started a military operation to eliminate nationalist citizens. This operation was known as Operation Searchlight. It triggered massive civil disobedience by civilians which ended on 16th December after the surrender of the military. Hence, the correct answer is "16th December 1971".

q: Who was the fifth of the ten Sikh gurus?
subject: history
exp: The fifth of the ten Sikh Gurus is Guru Arjan Dev. He became the Guru of the Sikh community in 1581 following the passing of his father, Guru Ram Das. List of the ten Sikh Gurus: Guru Number Name 1 Guru Nanak Dev 2 Guru Angad Dev | | SSC Guru Number Name 3 Guru Amar Das 4 Guru Ram Das 5 Guru Arjan Dev 6 Guru Har Gobind 7 Guru Har Rai 8 Guru Har Krishan 9 Guru Tegh Bahadur 10 Guru Gobind Singh Hence, the correct answer is “Guru Arjan Dev”.

q: The Act that transferred the power from the British East India Company to the British Crown in India was:
subject: history
exp: After the revolt of 1857 , the British Parliament passed a new Act in 1858 and transferred the powers of the East India Company to the British Crown in order to ensure a more responsible management of Indian affairs. A member of the British Cabinet was appointed Secretary of State for India and made responsible for all matters related to the governance of India. He was given a council to advise him, called the India Council. The Governor-General of India was given the title of Viceroy, that is, a personal representative of the Crown. Through these measures the British government accepted direct responsibility for ruling India. Hence, the correct answer is "Government of India Act, 1858".

q: In which of the following cities is the tomb of Rani of Jhansi situated?
subject: history
exp: The tomb of Rani of Jhansi is situated in the Phool Bagh Maidan Gwalior, Madhya Pradesh. Jhansi ki Rani, also known by the name Rani Lakshmibai, was a brave ruler of the princely kingdom of Jhansi and she became a national heroine and was seen as the epitome of female bravery in India. Samadhi of Rani of Jhansi is a small park where there is a memorial in the form of a statue of Maharani Lakshmi Bai on horseback. Hence, the correct answer is "Gwalior".

q: In which year did the Third Round Table Conference take place?
subject: history
exp: The Third Round Table Conference took place in 1932. This conference was a continuation of the series of discussions aimed at finding a solution to the constitutional crisis in India and devising a plan for India's future governance. It brought together Indian leaders, British politicians, and representatives of the British government to discuss India's political future. However, unlike the previous two Round Table Conferences, the Third Round Table Conference was largely unsuccessful as the British government was not willing to make significant concessions to the Indian demand for self-governance. As a result, the conference ended without any major agreements, and the Indian independence movement gained momentum. Hence, the correct answer is "1932".

q: The Gol Gumbaz (Gumbaz) of_________ is the mausoleum of Muhammad Adil Shah.
subject: history
exp: Gol Gumbaz is the mausoleum of king Mohammad Adil Shah , the sultan of Bijapur. This tomb is located in Bijapur , Karnataka. It is the second largest dome ever built . It was built by Mohammad Adil Shah. It was completed by Architect Yakut of Dabul in 1656 It is known as the Taj Mahal of South India. It is known for its whispering gallery in which whispers get amplified again and again. Hence, the correct answer is "Bijapur".

q: In which of the following locations was the Quit India Movement launched by Mahatma Gandhi in 1942 ?
subject: history
exp: The Quit India Movement was launched at Mumbai's Gowalia Tank Maidan also known as August Kranti Maidan, Bombay by Mahatma Gandhi on 8 August 1942 during World War II asking for an end to British rule in India. The movement started in a speech in Bombay where Mahatma Gandhi gave the slogan ‘Do or Die’. Shivaji Park also known as the Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj Park is a public park situated in Dadar, Mumbai and is the largest park in the island city. The Jallianwala Bagh massacre which is also known as the Amritsar massacre took place on 13 April 1919. Pragati Maidan is a venue for large exhibitions and conventions in the city of New Delhi, India. Hence, the correct answer is “August Kranti Maidan”.

q: Which of the following statements is correct with reference to the Karachi Session of the Congress (1931)?
o: Mahatma Gandhi presided over the session
The Khilafat movement was launched at this event
subject: history
exp: The Karachi Session of the Indian National Congress was held from March 26 to 31, 1931. This session was significant as it marked the end of the Second Round Table Conference and the election of Mahatma Gandhi to represent the Congress. Sardar Patel presided over the session. During the session, the congress adopted a resolution on fundamental rights and economic policy, outlining the social, cultural, and political agenda of the Party. The highlight of the session was the endorsement of the Gandhi-Irwin Pact, a political agreement between Mahatma Gandhi and the British government, which aimed to improve the political situation in India. The Karachi Session was a crucial turning point in India's struggle for independence and set the stage for future negotiations between the Indian National Congress and the British government. Hence, the correct answer is "The Gandhi- Irwin Pact was ratified".

q: In which year was the Hindustan Socialist Republican Association (HSRA) founded?
subject: history
exp: Hindustan Socialist Republican Association(HSRA) was established in 1928 at Feroz Shah Kotla in New Delhi. It was a revolutionary organization previously known as Hindustan Republican Army. It was founded by Ram Prasad Bismil and other associated members were Chandrashekhar Azad, Ashfaqulla Khan, Bhagat Singh, Sukhdev Thappar and Jogesh Chandra Chatterjee. The revolutionary activities of HSRA were : Kakori conspiracy, killing of John Saunders, Central Assembly Bombing Case, conducting robberies and raids . Hence , the correct answer is "1928".

q: Sir Thomas Roe came as an official ambassador from King James I of England to which Mughal emperor's court?
subject: history
exp: Sir Thomas Roe visited the court of Emperor Jahangir as an ambassador of King James I. He successfully obtained permission for trade and was instrumental in establishing factories at Agra, Ahmedabad, and Broach. This diplomatic mission laid the foundation for stronger economic ties between England and the Mughal Empire during that period. Roe's efforts contributed to the development of trade relations and cultural exchange between the two nations. Jahangir's openness to diplomatic engagement facilitated the establishment of English trading posts in key Mughal cities. Hence, the correct answer is "Jahangir."

q: The Indian National Congress session of September 1920 was held at______.
subject: history
exp: The special session of the Indian National Congress was held at Calcutta between the 4th and 9th of September 1920, under the presidency of Lala Lajpat Rai. Instigated by the British Government’s attitude towards the Khilafat movement and the Jallianwala Bagh massacre, a special session was organized specifically to pass the resolution of the Non- cooperation movement with a demand for Swaraj. The resolution was passed with a great majority. Hence, the correct answer is "Calcutta".

q: Who among the following conceived the Kakori train robbery in 1925?
o: Manmath Nath Gupta and Matangini Hazra
Ram Prasad Bismil and Ashfaqullah Khan
subject: history
exp: Ram Prasad Bismil and Ashfaqullah Khan conceived the Kakori train robbery in 1925 and this robbery occurred during the Indian Independence Movement against the British Indian Government between Kakori and near Lucknow on 9 August 1925. This incident has been termed "Kakori Train Action" by the Government in December 2022. Baikuntha Shukla was hanged for murdering Phanindra Nath Ghosh who had become a government approver which led to hanging of Bhagat Singh, Rajguru and Sukhdev. Hence, the correct answer is "Ram Prasad Bismil and Ashfaqullah Khan".

q: The Gadar Party was formed in the year _____.
subject: history
exp: Ghadar Party was formed in the year 1913 by Lala Hardayal. Initially it was named the Pacific Coast Hindustan Association and was formed on 15 July 1913 in the United States under the leadership of Lala Har Dayal, Baba Jawala Singh, Sant Baba Wasakha Singh Dadehar , Santokh Singh and Sohan Singh Bhakna as its president. Hence, the correct answer is “1913”.

q: _____ renounced his knighthood in protest for Jalianwalla Bagh mass killing.
subject: history
exp: Rabindranath Tagore renounced his knighthood in protest for Jallianwala Bagh mass killing. Mahatma Gandhi surrendered his Kaiser- i-Hind medal and his was a message that they wanted nothing to do with the cruel Britishers after the Jallianwala Bagh massacre. Shivaji Rao Holkar Bahadur XII was the Maharaja of Indore belonging to the Holkar dynasty, Marathas and was the son of Tukojirao Holkar II and Maharani Shrimant Akhand Soubhagyavati Parvati Bai Sahib. Sir Surendra Nath Banerjee, often known as Rashtraguru, was an Indian nationalist leader during the British Rule and he founded a nationalist organization called the Indian National Association to bring Hindus and Muslims together for political action. Jamsetjee Jejeebhoy was an Indian-Parsi merchant and philanthropist, later a British knight and baronet and made a huge fortune in cotton and the opium trade with China. Hence, the correct answer is “Rabindranath Tagore”.

q: Which leader gave us the C R Formula?
subject: history
exp: The "C R Formula" is most likely a reference to the C. R. Formula, which was proposed by Indian leader C. Rajagopalachari. He was a freedom fighter, politician, and statesman in India and served as the Governor-General of India from 1948 to 1950. C.Rajagopalachari, popularly known as Rajaji, was a prominent Indian independence activist and politician. He was a close associate of Mahatma Gandhi and was one of the leaders of the Indian National Congress. After independence, he served as the Chief Minister of Madras Presidency and later as the Governor-General of India, the only Indian to hold the office. He was also a noted writer and playwright, having authored several books including the Indian versions of several Shakespearean plays. Hence, the correct answer is "C. Rajagopalachari".

q: The Swaraj Party in 1923 was founded by ________.
o: Aruna Asaf Ali and Subhash Chandra Bose
Bhimrao Ambedkar and Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel
subject: history
exp: The Swaraj Party or the Congress-Khilafat Swarajya Party was formed within the Congress by Chittaranjan Das (CR Das) and Motilal Nehru on 1 January 1923. Swaraj Party was formed after the Gaya session of Congress in December 1922, which was presided over by CR Das. CR Das was the President and Motilal Nehru was the Secretary of the Swaraj Party. Hence, the correct answer is "Motilal Nehru and Chittaranjan Das".

q: Who was the then Governor-General of British India, when 'Sati Pratha' became illegal and punishable?
subject: history
exp: Lord William Bentick was the Governor General of British India when ‘Sati Pratha’ became illegal and punishable. Sati was very dominant in some areas of South Asia in which a widow was burnt alive with her deceased husband’s funeral pyre. Governor William Bentick passed Bengal Sati Regulation on December 4,1829 which made this practice illegal in India. William Bentick was known as ‘Father of Modern Western Education in India.’ Lord Cornwallis is known for his Permanent Settlement system of Bengal and known as Father of Civil Services in India. Lord Wellesely introduced the system of Subsidiary Alliance. Warren Hastings was first Governor- General of India. Hence, the correct answer is "Lord William Bentinck".

q: Who among the following scholars is associated with the Tibet-home theory of the Aryans?
subject: history
exp: Swami Dayanand Saraswati propounded the theory of the original home of the Aryans. According to the theory, the original home of the Aryans was Tibet. It was believed that the sun and fire were worshipped in Tibet due to extreme cold. Flora and fauna mentioned in Rig-Veda were found in Tibet, according to this theory. This view has been expounded by Dayanand Saraswati in the Satyarth Prakash. Hence, the correct answer is "Dayanand Saraswati."

q: Who was the Governor General of Bengal during the 3rd Anglo-Mysore war?
subject: history
exp: Cornwallis was the Governor General of Bengal during the 3rd Anglo-Mysore war. The war was fought between Tipu Sultan and Lord Cornwallis from 1790 to 1792.
