A treaty was signed by Tipu and through which he surrendered half of his territory to the allies. The treaty is known as Treaty of Seringapatam. Cornwallis introduced the Act of Permanent settlement. Governor General(tenure) Works John Shore (1793-1798) He introduced a policy of non-intervention. Warren Hastings (1772- 17850 He ended the dual system of government . First Governor General of Bengal. John Macpherson(17 85-1786) Did nothing to stop the gross corruption indulged in by company’s officials. Hence , the correct answer is "2nd Earl Cornwallis".

q: Who among the following was NOT associated with the revolt of 1857?
subject: history
exp: The Revolt of 1857 was a significant event in India's history, marked by a widespread uprising against British rule. It started as a mutiny of sepoys, or Indian soldiers in the British East India Company's army, in the town of Meerut on May 10, 1857, and soon spread across northern and central India. The rebellion was fueled by several factors, including cultural, economic, and political grievances, as well as a growing sense of Indian nationalism. Important personalities who participated from various parts of India included Begum Hazrat Mahal, Kuwar Singh, Nana Saheb, Rani Laxmi Bai, Mangal Pandey etc. However, Bhagat Singh contributed to national freedom in the 1900s. Hence, the correct answer is "Bhagat Singh".

q: Which of the following Viceroys of India addressed the Bengal famine of 1943 by ordering the army to distribute relief supplies to the starving rural Bengalis?
subject: history
exp: Lord Linlithgow addressed the Bengal famine of 1943 by ordering the army to distribute relief supplies to the starving rural Bengalis, and from 1936 to 1943, he was Governor-General and Viceroy of India. Mountbatten came up with Dickie Bird plan in May 1947, under which he proposed that the provinces be declared independent successor states and then be allowed to choose whether to join the constituent assembly or not. Lord Wavell announced a plan on 4 June 1945 for a new Executive Council in which all members except the Viceroy and the Commander-in-Chief would be Indians. Lord Willington convinced the Congress to join the second Round Table Conference, containing the second civil disobedience movement, and helped to shape the Government of India Bill, 1935. Hence, the correct answer is "Lord Linlithgow".

q: Freedom fighter, social reformer, and feminist Savitribai Phule hailed from which of the following states of India?
subject: history
exp: Savitribai Phule was born on January 3, 1831, in Maharashtra. She was married to Jyotirao Phule, who played a crucial role in her education. Jyotirao Phule aimed to elevate the status of women and, therefore, decided to educate Savitribai. As a social reformer, she worked tirelessly for women's empowerment and education. After completing her education, Savithribai Phule opened a girl's school at Vishrambag, Pune. She became the first female teacher in India, marking a significant milestone in women's education. Savitribai Phule passed away in 1897 due to the Bubonic Plague while helping those affected by the epidemic. Hence, the correct answer is "Maharashtra."

q: In which city was the Jhanda Satyagraha or Flag Satyagraha of 1923 held?
subject: history
exp: Flag Satyagraha was held in Jabalpur and Nagpur in 1923. It was a peaceful civil disobedience which was focused on hoisting the national flag . Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel, Dr. Rajendra Prasad, Vinoba Bhave participated in this satyagrah. Jawahar Lal Nehru hoisted the National flag on the banks of river Ravi in Lahore. Hence, the correct answer is "Nagpur".

q: In which of the following years was the Asiatic Society at Calcutta (Kolkata) founded by Sir William Jones?
subject: history
exp: The Asiatic Society was established in Calcutta by Sir William Jones in the year 1784. Located in Kolkata, it served as a hub for various literary and scientific endeavours. The institution aimed to be a comprehensive centre for Asian studies, encompassing all aspects related to man and nature across the continent. It was Warren Hastings who initiated the formation of the Bengal Asiatic Society. Hence, the correct answer is "1784."

q: Who among the following assassinated Sir William Hutt Curzon Wyllie in London?
subject: history
exp: Sir William Hutt Curzon Wyllie was assassinated on 1 July 1909, in London. The perpetrator of the assassination was Madan Lal Dhingra, an Indian revolutionary. Dhingra was a member of India House in London at the time. Curzon Wyllie was a British Indian army officer and a political aide-de-camp to the Secretary of State for India. Dhingra claimed his act was a patriotic response to the injustices perpetrated by the British Government in India. Hence, the correct answer is "Madan Lal Dhingra."

q: Which of the following Acts introduced federal features and provincial autonomy in the legislature and also made provisions for the distribution of legislative powers between the Centre and the provinces?
subject: history
exp: The Government of India Act, of 1935, implemented federal features in the legislative structure. It granted significant autonomy to the provinces within British India. This act delineated the distribution of legislative powers between the central government and the provincial governments. Through its provisions, it aimed at restructuring the governance system in British India. Hence, the correct answer is "The Government of India Act, 1935."

q: Who among the following was one of the leaders of the Santhal rebellion?
subject: history
exp: The Santhal Rebellion occurred during Lord Dalhousie's tenure from 1848 to 1856 in the Rajmahal hills of Jharkhand. Sidhu Manjhi was one of the prominent leaders of the Santhal rebellion. The rebellion arose due to discontent with British revenue systems, usury practices, and the zamindari system. Sidhu, along with his brother Kanhu Murmu, organized around 10,000 Santhals to revolt against British rule. The rebellion was eventually suppressed, leading to the establishment of the Santhal Pargana and special laws for their protection. Hence, the correct answer is "Sidhu Manjihi."

q: Veer Surendra Sai was a freedom fighter from:
subject: history
exp: Veer Surendra Sai was born on 23 January 1809 in the Bengal Presidency. He was a prominent freedom fighter hailing from the region that is now known as Odisha. Sai played a significant role in the Indian independence movement, leading numerous rebellions against British rule. His efforts and sacrifices earned him the status of a revered figure in the history of India's struggle for freedom. Hence, the correct answer is "Odisha".

q: Mughal Emperor Shah Alam appointed the British East India Company as the Diwan of the province of Bengal in the year:
subject: history
exp: On 12 August 1765, following the Battle of Buxar, the Mughal emperor appointed the East India Company as the Diwan of Bengal. This appointment was formalized through the Treaty of Allahabad, signed between the Mughal Emperor and Robert Clive of the East India Company. The Treaty of Allahabad marked the beginning of British involvement in Indian politics and the establishment of their rule. As Diwan, the East India Company gained significant financial control, administering taxes and revenue in Bengal, Bihar, and Orissa. Robert Clive's victory in the Battle of Buxar solidified the Company's position as a dominant force in Indian affairs. Hence, the correct answer is "1765."

q: In which of the following years was the Second Round Table Conference in London held?
subject: history
exp: In 1931, the Second Round Table Conference took place in London. Gandhi and the Indian National Congress participated, and it lasted from September 7 to December 1, 1931. Jawahar Lal Nehru did not attend the Second Round Conference Table. From 17 November to 24 December 1932, the third Round Table Conference took place. From 12 November 1930 to 19 January 1931, the first Round Table Conference met. All three round table discussions were witnessed by Tej Bahadur and B.R. Ambedkar. Hence, the correct answer is ''1931''.

q: In which of the following years did the revolt in the countryside of the Bombay Deccan occur?
subject: history
exp: In 1875, there was a revolt in the Bombay Deccan region. The Deccan Riots of 1875 focussed on conditions of debt peonage to moneylenders. The moneylenders' contracts, orders, and other papers were sought after and destroyed as its primary goal. Mumbai serves as India's business, commercial, and leisure hub. Among all Indian towns, it has the most wealthy and billionaires. Hence, the correct answer is "1875".

q: In which of the following years was the Indian Independence Act passed by the British Parliament?
subject: history
exp: The Indian Independence Act, of 1947 received royal assent and entered into force on 18th July 1947. This act partitioned India into two independent dominions India and Pakistan. It was decided to grant independence to India and Pakistan with effect from 15th August 1947. The act also marked the end of British suzerainty over the princely states, allowing them to decide whether to join India, and Pakistan or remain independent. Over 560 princely states ultimately decided to merge with India. Hence, the correct answer is "1947."

q: In which of the following years was the Indian Association established?
subject: history
exp: In 1876, the Indian Association was founded. It was established in British India as the country's first openly nationalist group. In 1876, Ananda Mohan Bose and Surendranath Banerjee established it. Its goal is to progress the political, intellectual, and financial well-being of the populace through all appropriate methods. It drew social leaders and educated Indians from all over the nation. It developed into a crucial venue for India's freedom movements. Hence, the correct answer is "1876".

q: What was the name of the women's regiment in the Indian National Army founded by Subhash Chandra Bose?
subject: history
exp: The Rani of Jhansi Regiment was the women's regiment of the Indian National Army, established during World War II. Led by Captain Lakshmi Swaminathan, the unit was formed in July 1943 with volunteers from the expatriate Indian population in Southeast Asia. Subhash Chandra Bose announced the creation of this regiment on 12 July 1943, marking a significant stride in women's involvement in the armed forces. The Rani of Jhansi Regiment remains notable as one of the all-female combat regiments during the Second World War, operating on the side of Indian nationalists. Hence, the correct answer is "Rani of Jhansi Regiment."

q: Alivardi Khan was a Nawab of ______.
subject: history
exp: Alivardi Khan was Bengal's Nawab. He is also renowned for defeating the Maratha Empire in the Battle of Burdwan. He overthrew the Nawabs of the Nasiri family and took control for himself. For the years 1740 to 1756, he served as Bengal's Nawab. Hence, the correct answer is "Bengal".

q: The Indian Mutiny began from Meerut on 10 May 1857 and ended in:
subject: history
exp: The Indian Mutiny, also known as the Sepoy Mutiny or the First War of Independence, started in Meerut on 10 May 1857. It was a widespread but ultimately unsuccessful rebellion against British rule in India. The rebellion spread to key cities like Delhi, Agra, Kanpur, and Lucknow. Indian troops (sepoys) in the service of the British East India Company led the uprising. The Indian Mutiny came to an end in Gwalior on 20 June 1858 when Indian troops were defeated by the British forces. Hence, the correct answer is "Gwalior on 20 June 1858."

q: Who among the following was one of the founders of the Hindustan Republican Association?
subject: history
exp: One of the leaders of the Hindustan Republican Association was Ram Prasad Bismil. It was a revolutionary group that upheld socialism and Marxism. It was established by Jogesh Chandra Chatterjee, Sachindra Nath Bakshi, Sachindra Nath Sanyal, Ashfaqulla Khan, and Ram Prasad Bismil. An ardent participant in India's independence battle, Ram Prasad Bismil was a freedom fighter. Lala Lajpat Rai was crucial to the fight for Indian independence. The Chittagong Armory Raid of 1930 is best remembered for having been led by Surya Sen. Hence, the correct answer is ''Ram Prasad Bismil.''

q: Who among the following laid the foundations of the Indian National Movement by founding the Indian Association at Calcutta in 1876 ?
subject: history
exp: By establishing the Indian Association in Calcutta in 1876, Surendranath Banerjee set the foundation for the Indian National Movement. He was one of the Indian National Congress' original members. The first openly nationalist group to be established in British India was the Indian Association. It drew social leaders and intelligent Indians from all over the nation. The primary goals were to advance the people's political, academic, and financial progress through all legal means. Dadabhai Naoroji is also known as the Grand Old Man of India and Unofficial Ambassador of India. Hence, the correct answer is Surendranath Banerjee

q: Swami Vivekananda championed the supremacy of Vedantic philosophy in the Chicago (the US) Conference of World Religions held in the year_____.
subject: history
exp: The Parliament of Religions opened on September 11, 1893, where Swami Vivekananda delivered his influential address. Swami Vivekananda captivated the audience with his plea for religious tolerance and an end to fanaticism during the Parliament sessions. Over the subsequent weeks, thousands of attendees flocked to listen to Swami Vivekananda, making the Parliament a significant event in interfaith dialogue. The World Religions Conference (WRC) is renowned for bringing together scholars from various religious backgrounds to discuss common topics from their respective traditions. The WRC promotes values such as tolerance, peace, cooperation, and understanding, and has grown in terms of organization, content quality, and attendance over its 40-year history. Hence, the correct answer is "1893."

q: In which of the following years was the Bombay Reorganization Act passed?
subject: history
exp: The state of Bombay was initially included in Part A, a former governorate province of British India as per the First Schedule in the 1949 Constitution. Bombay State encompassed a large portion of modern Gujarat and parts of present-day Maharashtra and Karnataka. The States Reorganization Act aimed to redistribute territories, transferring some Kannada-speaking regions to Mysore State and expanding Bombay's areas to include Marathi-speaking Marathwada and Vidarbha, as well as Gujarat- speaking Saurashtra and Kutch. On 1st May 1960, the Bombay Reorganization Act was passed, marking it as Appointment Day, when the state was bifurcated into Gujarat and Maharashtra. Hence, the correct answer is "1960."

q: Chandernagore (Chandannagar) was a ___ colony captured by the British Navy on 23 March 1757.
subject: history
exp: The French settlement of Chandernagore (Chandannagar) was captured by the British Navy on March 23, 1757. By getting approval from the Nawab of Bengal, Ibrahim Khan, to set up a trading post on the banks of the river Hooghly, Chandannagar was founded as a French settlement in 1673. In 1688, it was settled permanently by the French, and Joseph Francois Dupleix was named the city's governor in 1730. On March 23, 1757, the British Navy seized it. After being returned to the French in 1763, the British took it back in 1794. The city and a 3-square-mile enclave of the neighbouring land were restored to France in 1816. It was administered under the political authority of French India until 1950. Hence the correct answer is "French".

q: In which of the following years was the All- India Harijan Sevak Sangh founded?
subject: history
exp: All India Harijan Sevak Sangh was established in the year 1932. In order to end untouchability, Mahatma Gandhi founded this nonprofit organisation. The All India Anti-Untouchability League is its other name. It works for Harijan or Dalit people and upliftment of the Depressed Class of India. It has branches in 26 states across India and its headquarters are at Kingsway Camp in Delhi. Hence the correct answer is "1932".

q: Who among the following freedom fighters wrote the poem 'Vande Mataram' in 1875?
subject: history
exp: The poem "Vande Mataram" was written by Bankim Chandra Chatterjee in 1875. He is recognized as a pivotal character in Bengal's and the Indian subcontinent's literary revival. Rabindranath Tagore sang this song for the first time at the Indian National Congress gathering in 1896. The first non-European author to receive the Nobel Prize in Literature was Rabindranath Tagore. An important figure in the early 20th- century Indian freedom struggle was Bhagat Singh. Along with Sri Aurobindo, Bipin Chandra Pal was a key figure in the creation of the Swadeshi movement. Hence, the correct answer is "Bankim Chandra Chatterjee".

q: In which of the following years was the origin and enactment of the Indian Age of Consent Act passed?
subject: history
exp: The Indian Age of Consent Act was enacted in British India on 19 March 1891. This legislation raised the age of consent for sexual intercourse from ten to twelve years for all girls in all jurisdictions. The Act, also known as Act X of 1891, amended the Indian Penal Code and the Code of Criminal Procedure, Section 375, 1882. Despite opposition from figures like Sir Romesh Chunder Mitter, who argued it interfered with the orthodox Hindu code, it received support from Indian reformers like Behramji Malabari and women's social organizations. Hence, the correct answer is "1891."

q: C Rajagopalachari led the Salt Satyagraha in which of the following states?
subject: history
exp: In Tamil Nadu, the Salt Satyagraha was held by C. Rajagopalachari. He served as India's last governor- general and also the first Indian-born governor-general in India. He was among the first individuals to receive India's greatest national honor. He established the Swatantra Party and served as India's last governor-general. Tamil Nadu ranks sixth in terms of population and is the tenth-largest state in terms of area in India. It is both the most urbanized and one of the most industrialized regions in India. Hence, the correct answer is "Tamil Nadu".

q: In which of the following years did the Kakori Conspiracy case take place?
subject: history
exp: The Kakori Conspiracy case took place in the year 1925. The Kakori Conspiracy case was an important event during India's struggle for independence against British rule. It involved an attempted train robbery on the 9th of August 1925, at Kakori near Lucknow, Uttar Pradesh. The freedom fighters involved in the incident were part of the Hindustan Republican Association (HRA), a revolutionary organization. Hence, the correct answer is ‘1925.’

q: Who among the following along with Motilal Nehru was Instrumental in the creation of the Swaraj Party?
subject: history
exp: Together with Motilal Nehru, Chittaranjan Das played a crucial role in the formation of the Swaraj Party. He is known as Deshbandhu, which means "Friend of the Nation." One of the movement's primary leaders was Bipin Chandra Pal. In India, he is referred to as the Father of Revolutionary Thoughts. Subhash Chandra Bose, a charismatic orator with exceptional leadership abilities, was regarded as the most significant independence warrior. The Indian National Association was a nationalist group that was established by Surendranath Banerjee. Hence, the correct answer is "Chittaranjan Das".

q: Who among the following Sikh Gurus founded the Tarn Taran Sahib?
subject: history
exp: Tarn Taran Sahib is situated in the Majha region of Punjab, India. It serves as the district headquarters and houses the municipal council of Tarn Taran district. Gurdwara Sri Tarn Taran Sahib, a significant Sikh pilgrimage centre, is located in the heart of the city. The town of Tarn Taran was established by the fifth Sikh Guru, Sri Guru Arjan Dev (1563–1606). Guru Arjan Dev laid the foundation of the Tarn Taran Sahib Temple, making it a prominent site for Sikh worship. Hence, the correct answer is "Guru Arjan Dev."

q: In which of the following years did Japan invade India resulting in the Battle of Imphal?
subject: history
exp: Invasion of India by Japan led to the 1944 Battle of Imphal. The Japanese suffered their biggest loss to that point at Kohima and Imphal. Between 8 March and 18 July 1944, the Battle of Imphal was contested. The conflict, which took place in the South-East Asian region of World War II, marked a turning moment in the Burma war. Hence, the correct answer is '1944'.

q: How many delegates participated in the second session of the Indian National Congress?
subject: history
exp: On 28 December 1885, 72 delegates participated in the first session of Indian National Congress at Gokuldas Tejpal Sanskrit College, Bombay. The second session of the Congress took place under the leadership of Dadabhai Naoroji in Calcutta. The number of delegates had increased to 434 . Hence, the correct answer is " 434."

q: Which of the following led English company in Battle of Plassey (1757)?
subject: history
exp: Robert Clive's East India Company men faced Siraj-Ud-Daulah in the Battle of Plassey. The Nawab of Bengal was named Siraj- Ud-Daulah. The flagrant misuse of trade privileges by EIC officials infuriated Siraj. The ongoing wrongdoing by EIC against Siraj-Ud-Daulah led to the battle of Plassey in 1757. On the banks of the Bhagirathi-Hooghly River, about 160 kilometers north of Calcutta, near the small village of Plassey (Palashi), the soldiers gathered. The Battle of Plassey was fought on June 23, 1757. The fight ended in eleven hours, and Siraj-Ud-Daulah lost and fled. Hence, the correct answer is ''Robert Clive''.

q: How many delegates attended the first session of Congress in 1885?
subject: history
exp: On the 28th of December in the year 1885, the inaugural session of the Indian National Congress was convened at the Gokuldas Tejpal Sanskrit College in the city of Bombay. There were a total of 72 delegates present. Following W. C. Bonnerjee of Calcutta's victory in the election for president, Hume was appointed to the position of General Secretary. Hence, the correct answer is "72".

q: Ishwarchandra Vidyasagar set up school for girls at ______.
subject: history
exp: Reformer Ishwarchandra Vidyasagar of Bengal actively brought up women's issues. He actively supported girls' education because he thought that the root of all of their issues was a lack of education. Assisted by an Englishman by the name of Bethune, he established numerous schools specifically for girls. He took a strong stance against polygamy and child marriage. He fervently supported widow remarriage. His vigorous campaigning for support led to the passage of the Widows' Remarriage Act in 1856, which made widow remarriages lawful. Hence, the correct answer is "Calcutta".

q: Who among the following persons was associated with the Chittagong Armoury Raid?
subject: history
exp: The Chittagong Armoury Raid took place on April 18, 1930. The Indian revolutionaries like Ganesh Ghosh, Lokenath Bal, Nirmal Sen, Ambika Chakraborty, Naresh Roy, Sasanka Datta, Ardhendu Dastidar, Harigopal Bal, Tarakeswar Dastidar, Ananta Singh, Jiban Ghoshal, Anand Gupta, Pritilata Waddedar, and Kalpana Dutta led by Surya Sen decided to raid the armoury of police and auxiliary forces from the Chittagong armoury in the Bengal presidency. Twelve revolutionaries and 80 British Army soldiers were killed in the bloody confrontation that ensued on April 22, 1930, atop Jalalabad hill when hundreds of British army soldiers caught up with them. Hence, the correct answer is "Ardhendu Dastidar".

q: Who among the following freedom fighters hoisted the Indian flag at the Gowalia Tank Maidan in Mumbai during the Quit India Movement?
subject: history
exp: Aruna Asaf Ali was an Indian publisher, educator, and political activist. She was a prominent member of the Indian independence movement and is best known for raising the Indian national flag at the 1942 Quit India Movement in Bombay's Gowalia Tank Maidan. August 8, 1942, marked the beginning of the Quit India Movement. India celebrates August Kranti Din on August 8 of each year. The Quit Indian Movement was directly caused by the Cripps Mission's failure. Hence, the correct answer is "Aruna Asaf Ali".

q: In the context of the Indian Freedom Movement, which of the following years is correctly paired with the event that took place that year?
o: 1915 – Mahatma Gandhi returned from South Africa
1944 – The Cripps Mission was sent by the British government to India to obtain Indian cooperation for the British war efforts in the 2nd World War
1945 – The All India Congress Committee met in Bombay and ratified the 'Quit India' resolution
subject: history
exp: Mahatma Gandhi returned to India on January 9, 1915, after over 21 years in South Africa. He arrived at the Apollo Bunder in Bombay and received a hero's welcome. Gandhi's return from South Africa is commemorated on Pravasi Bharatiya Divas, observed on January 9 by India. The Cripps Mission was sent in 1942, not 1944, to seek Indian cooperation during World War II. The First Round Table Conference took place in 1930-1931, not 1936. The All India Congress Committee ratified the 'Quit India' resolution in 1942, not 1945. Hence, the correct answer is "1915 – Mahatma Gandhi returned from South Africa."

q: Which of the following pairs of Indian revolutionaries and organisations is CORRECTLY paired?
o: M Veera Raghavachari – Poona Sarvajanik Sabha
Mahadev Govind Ranade – Madras Mahajana Sabha
subject: history
exp: In 1885, Pherozeshah Mehta, Badruddin Tyabji, and K. T. Telang played a major role in the Bombay Presidency Association's establishment. The Bombay Presidency Association represented Indian interests and hosted the first meeting of the Indian National Congress in Bombay at the end of 1885. The Madras Mahajana Sabha was founded by Ramaswami Mudaliar and P. Anandacharlu in 1884. Dadabhai Naoroji established the East India Association in London in 1866 after working with Indians and former British officials. In Poona, the Poona Sarvajanik Sabha was established in 1870. S. H. Chiplunkar, Ganesh Vasudeo Joshi, and Mahadev Govind Ranade founded it. Hence, the correct answer is "Badruddin Tyabji – Bombay Presidency Association".

q: In which of the following years was the civil disobedience campaign completely ceased?
subject: history
exp: Gandhi launched the Civil Disobedience Movement on March 12, 1930, by participating in the illustrious Dandi Salt March, in which he defied the salt prohibition. When Mahatma Gandhi finished his mass satyagraha on July 14, 1933, the Civil Disobedience Movement was put on hold. On April 7, 1934, the movement came to an end totally. The movement of civil disobedience expanded to include boycotting foreign products, not filing taxes, and skipping work and education. The British imprisoned Jawaharlal Nehru and Mahatma Gandhi because they were disturbed by this movement. Hence, the correct answer is "1934".

q: Which of the following events occurred first during the Indian National Movement?
subject: history
exp: The proposal for Bengal's partition in 1905 came from Lord Curzon. In July 1905, he issued the order for Bengal's partition, which became effective in October of the same year. In 1928, the Simon Commission came to India. It traveled to India in order to review the 1919 Government of India Act. In 1918, Gandhiji began the Satyagraha in Kheda, Gujarat, to aid the peasants who were unable to pay the land tax because their crops had failed. Additionally, from March 12, 1930, to April 6, 1930, he led the Dandi March, also known as the Salt Satyagraha, from Sabarmati Ashram to Dandi on the Gujarat coast. Hence, the correct answer is "Partition of Bengal".

q: “All India Muslim League” was established in 1906 at ______.
subject: history
exp: In the year 1906, Dhaka became the location of the founding of the All-India Muslim League (AIML). The position of president was first filled by Aga Khan. Alongside Mohsin, Viqar-ul-Mulk was promoted to the position of joint secretary. Karachi was host to the inaugural meeting of the All India Muslim League, which took place on December 29, 1907. Hence, the correct answer is 'Dacca'.

q: Established in 1867 in Bombay, the ________ sought to remove caste restrictions, abolish child marriage, and encourage the education of women, etc.
subject: history
exp: Dr. Atmaram Pandurang founded the Prarthana Samaj in Bombay in 1867. It was a Brahmo Samaj offshoot. It was a movement for justice and reform within Hinduism. It gained new vigor when M.G. Ranade and R.G. Bhandarkar joined it in 1870. Mahadev Govind Ranade also ran the Deccan Education Society. A number of Prarthana Samaj members were previously involved in the Paramhansa Mandali. This Samaj advocated monotheism and condemned caste rigidities, idolatry, and priestly dominance. Hence, the correct answer is "Prarthana Samaj".

q: The British East India Company captured Pondicherry (Puducherry) from the French in the year_________.
subject: history
exp: Puducherry changed hands during the Anglo-French wars that lasted from 1742 to 1763. In 1761, the British "East India Company" took Puducherry from the French, and the Treaty of Paris in 1763 reinstated the French Company's rule. In 1761, the French were forced out of Pondicherry (Puducherry) by the British East India Company. The French "East India Company" was permitted to maintain its settlements in Pondicherry, Mahe, Yanam, Karaikal, and Chandernagore after the British "East India Company" seized power in the late 1850s. Hence, the correct answer is "1761".

q: In which year was the 'Lotteries Regulation Act' passed?
subject: history
exp: An important piece of law, the Lotteries Regulation Act, 1998, establishes specific guidelines for the central government's handling of lotteries in India. Additionally, subject to a few restrictions, it enables state governments to run and control lotteries. This legislation was passed on July 7th, 1998, and went into effect on October 2nd of the same year. Hence, the correct answer is "1998".

q: At which of the following jails was Khudiram Bose sent to the gallows?
subject: history
exp: By throwing bombs at the carriage that the District Judge, Mr. Kingsford, was going to ride in, Khudiram Bose and Prafulla attempted to kill him. Two British women died in the carriage when Mr. Kingsford was not there. Prafulla Chaki took his own life before the authorities could take him into custody. Khudiram Bose was detained before receiving a death sentence. At the Muzaffarpur Jail, which has been renamed in his honour, he was executed when he was just 18 years old. Hence, the correct answer is "Muzaffarpur".

q: After the ____________ , Mahatma Gandhi called off the Non-Cooperation Movement.
subject: history
exp: After the Chauri Chaura incident, Mahatma Gandhi called off the Non- Cooperation Movement. The Chauri Chaura incident took place on February 5, 1922, in the Chauri Chaura town of the Gorakhpur district in Uttar Pradesh, India. During the incident, a group of protestors clashed with the police, leading to the death of several police officers. In response to this violent turn of events, Mahatma Gandhi, who had been leading the Non-Cooperation Movement, decided to withdraw the movement. The Non-Cooperation Movement was a significant phase of the Indian independence struggle, aiming to protest against British colonial rule through nonviolent means. The Chauri Chaura incident was seen as a departure from the principles of nonviolence and civil disobedience that Gandhi advocated. Hence, the correct answer is "Chauri Chaura incidence."

q: The first English Factory in Bengal set up on the banks of river "Hugli" in _____ .
subject: history
exp: In the year 1651, the first English factory in the region of Bengal was established on the banks of the river Hugli. The East India Company was successful in persuading merchants to set up shop in the area around the factory. The factory was initially used as a trading post for the Company's goods, particularly textiles and spices. By the year 1696, the Company had begun construction of a fort around the Hugli village, which would eventually become the city of Calcutta. Hence, the correct answer is "1651."

q: Which of the following revolutionary groups' members assassinated Assistant Superintendent of Police John Saunders in 1927?
subject: history
exp: On December 17, 1927, members of the Hindustan Republican Association, including Bhagat Singh and Shivaram Rajguru, killed John Saunders, the assistant superintendent of police, after mistaking him for James A. Scott and shooting him in the head. Sukhdev Thapar and Chandrashekhar Azad were the ones who encouraged them to carry out this deed. Later on, Bhagat Singh, Sukhdev, and Rajguru were all put to death by hanging. Hence, the correct answer is " Hindustan Republican Association ."

q: The Hindu Marriage Act was enacted in the year_____.
subject: history
exp: The Hindu Marriage Act was passed by the Indian Parliament in 1955. During this period, the Hindu Code Bills also resulted in the passage of three further significant laws: The Hindu Succession Act (1956). The Hindu Minority and Guardianship Act (1956). The Hindu Adoptions and Maintenance Act (1956). The act was intended to secure the rights of marriage for the bride and groom who are Hindus and are bound under the sacred bond of marriage under any ceremony. The act can removed any distinction under the law of marriage of a young woman and a widow, and both are treated equally under this law. Hence the correct answer is "1955".

q: Realising the importance of maintaining the cleanliness of the water bodies, the Government of India passed the Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, _______, to safeguard our water resources.
subject: history
exp: A change in the physical, chemical, or biological properties of the air, water, or land that is unwelcome and might have an impact on people or other living things is called pollution. The Environment (Protection) Act was established by the Indian government in 1986 with the goal of preserving and enhancing the quality of the environment. Air pollution, land pollution, and water pollution are the three main types of environmental pollution that have an impact on the quality of the environment. The definition of water pollution is the introduction of chemicals (organic, inorganic, or biological) that cause the water's quality to decline and render it unsuitable for human use. The Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act was enacted by the Government of India in 1974 to protect the water resources from pollution since it was recognised how important it was to keep the water bodies clean. This legislation outlines the prevention, mitigation, and control of water pollution as well as the preservation or restoration of the nation's water's wholesomeness. Hence the correct answer is "1974".

q: The Revolutionary Socialist Party was established in ____.
subject: history
exp: On March 19, 1940, Tridib Chaudhuri established the Revolutionary Socialist Party, which later became known as the RSP. It was primarily organized as a political expression of the Anushilan Samiti, also known as the Liberation Movement in Bengal, which led to its formation. The Hindustan Socialist Republican Army is another source of inspiration for the organization. Hence, 1940 is the correct answer.

q: The Hindu College was established in Banaras in_______.
subject: history
exp: The Hindu College was founded in the year 1791 in the city of Benaras. A primary objective is to foster interest in the research of ancient Sanskrit literature that might be of value in the governance of the nation. Jonathan Duncan, who was a resident of the East India Company at the time, made the idea that led to the establishment of Olt, and Governor General Lord Cornwallis gave his permission. Hence, the correct answer is "1791".

q: The British Parliament transferred the powers of the East India Company to the British crown in _______.
subject: history
exp: The British Parliament transferred the powers of the East India Company to the British crown in 1858. This transfer of power marked the beginning of direct British rule in India and the end of the East India Company's control over the Indian subcontinent. The formal instrument for this transfer was the Government of India Act 1858. Following this transfer, India was governed by the British Crown through the Secretary of State for India. The Governor-General of India continued to serve as the head of the government in India, but now under the authority of the British monarch. Hence, the correct answer is "1858."

q: In which of the following sessions of the Indian National Congress did George Yule become the President in 1888?
subject: history
exp: In the fourth session of the INC, which was held in Allahabad in 1888, George Yule was elected president. He was the first non-Indian President of INC. He was a member of the business elite. He was the head of Calcutta's renowned Andrew Yule and Co. In addition, he had served as President of the Indian Chamber of Commerce and Sheriff of Calcutta. In Indian circles, Yule was well-known for his liberal ideas, broad perspective, and obvious empathy for Indian goals. Hence, the correct answer is "Allahabad".

q: After Mahatma Gandhi's release from prison in January 1931 , Congress leaders met at ________ to plan the future course of action.
subject: history
exp: Congress leaders convened in Allahabad in January 1931, following Mahatma Gandhi's release from prison, to strategize the future course of action. Gandhi Ji started the Dandi March from his Asharam in Sabarmati to manufacture his own salt in Dandi, in defiance of the salt law. Mahatma Gandhi started the Salt March, also called the Dandi March. It began on March 12, 1930, and took place in Sabarmati, close to Ahmedabad, for 24 days. Gandhiji and thousands of others were arrested while producing their own salt in opposition to British rule and the British government's attempt to legalize and tax the industry. In the end, it proved to be successful when the British freed the demonstrators from jail and allowed the populace to produce their own salt. Hence, the correct answer is "Allahabad".

q: With which of the following organisations was Lala Har Dayal associated?
subject: history
exp: In the USA, he established the Gadar Party in 1913. The following are just a few of his published works: "Thoughts in Education," "Social Conquest of Hindu Race," "Forty Four Months in Germany and Turkey," "Hints for Self Culture," "Bodhisatva Doctrines," etc. He passed away on March 4, 1939, in Philadelphia. India Post issued a commemorative stamp in 1987 in memory of Lala Har Dayal. Hence, "Ghadar Party" is the correct answer.

q: In 1600 , the East India company acquired a charter from which ruler?
subject: history
exp: Elizabeth I, the English queen, granted the company a royal charter, which allowed it to be founded on December 31, 1600. An English company was established to engage in trade with India and East and Southeast Asia. In 1608, the Company sent Captain Hawkins to Jahangir's court in the Mughal Empire to obtain permission to open a warehouse for goods in Surat. Jahangir granted the East India Company permission to open its first trading post at Surat in 1613. English-established business hubs in Broach, Ahmedabad, and Agra. Hence, the correct answer is "Queen Elizabeth-I ".

q: The Vernacular Press Act of 1878 was repealed during the tenure of Viceroy _________.
subject: history
exp: Lord Ripon repealed the Vernacular Press Act of 1881. The viceroy feared that the act may produce resentment among Indians. The act can become one of the catalysts giving rise to India's growing independence movement. Hence, the correct answer is "Lord Ripon."

q: Battle of Plassey was fought between English company and Nawab of Bengal in which year?
subject: history
exp: The Battle of Plassey was fought on June 23, 1757, between the British East India Company and the Nawab of Bengal, Siraj ud-Daulah. The battle was a decisive victory for the British, marking the beginning of British rule in India. The battle was fought at Plassey, a village in the Nadia district of West Bengal. Robert Clive, led the British and Mir Jafar led the Nawab. The British had a much smaller army than the Nawab, but they were better trained and equipped. They also had the support of the local Hindu rajas, who were unhappy with the Nawab's rule. Hence, the correct answer is "1757."

q: Subsidiary alliance treaty was imposed on Awadh in ______ .
subject: history
exp: The Subsidiary Alliance was a pact between the British East India Company and the Indian Princely states. As a result of this treaty, the Indian kingdoms ceded their authority over their territories to the English. Lord Wellesley, in the year 1801, coerced the Nawab of Awadh into accepting the Policy of Subsidiary Alliance. In 1801, Wellesley coerced the Nawab of Awadh into accepting the Policy of Subsidiary Alliance. The Nawab of Awadh was the first monarch to establish a secondary alliance with the British after the Battle of Buxar. However, the first to agree to a properly constructed subsidiary alliance was the Nizam of Hyderabad. Hence, the correct answer is "1801".

q: Which of the following statements was quoted by Subhash Chandra Bose?
o: Live as if you were to die tomorrow. Learn as if you were to live forever.
The best way to find yourself is to lose yourself in the service of others.
Give me blood and I will give you freedom.
First, they ignore you, then they laugh at you, then they fight you, then you win.
subject: history
exp: ‘Give me blood and I will give you freedom’, was quoted by Subhash Chandra Bose to the soldiers of Indian National Army. ‘First, they ignore you, then they laugh at you, then they fight you, then you win’: Mahatama Gandhi ‘The best way to find yourself is to lose yourself in the service of others’: Mahatma Gandhi ‘Live as if you were to die tomorrow. Learn as if you were to live forever’: Mahatama Gandhi Hence the correct answer is "Give me blood and I will give you freedom".

q: Which of the following revolts was led by Kanhu and Sidhu?
subject: history
exp: On June 30, 1855, two years prior to the Great Uprising of 1857, two brothers named Sidhu and Kanhu Murmu of the Santhal tribe planned the Santhal uprising. They were able to rally 10,000 Santhals to their cause and launch an uprising against the British. The indigenous peoples swore an oath to expel the British from their territory once and for all. Hence, the correct answer is "Santhal Revolt ."

q: Dadabhai Naoroji offered a scathing criticism of the economic impact of British rule throught his book entitled '____________'.
o: Poverty and Un - British Rule in India
subject: history
exp: The "Drain Theory" was first presented in Dadabhal Naoroji's book that was released in 1901 and titled "Poverty and Un-British Rule in India." He was the first person to remark that the lack of riches and prosperity in India was a result of colonial rule, which at the time was sapping the country's resources. Hence, the correct answer is "Poverty and Un - British Rule in India".

q: Who among the following was the only martyr of the Quit India phase of the freedom movement who was hanged?
subject: history
exp: An Indian-Assamese freedom fighter from Assam Kushal Konwar was the only martyr executed by hanging in India during the last phase of the Quit India Movement in 1942–1943. Because of his self-sacrifice for the sake of the Andhra cause, Indian revolutionary and liberation warrior Potti Sreeramulu is regarded as Amarajeevi in the Andhra region. Senapati Bapat, also known as Pandurang Mahadev Bapat, was an important participant in the Indian freedom struggle. Kanhaiyalal Maneklal Munshi, better known by his pen name Ghanshyam Vyas, was a socialist, politician, writer, and educationist from Gujarat state who was active in the Indian independence struggle. Hence the correct answer is "Kushal Konwar".

q: Partition of Bengal held in _____.
subject: history
exp: The Partition of Bengal was held in 1905. The Partition of Bengal was a territorial reorganization of the Bengal Presidency implemented by the authorities of the British Raj. The reorganization separated the largely Muslim eastern areas from the largely Hindu western areas. Announced on 20 July 1905 by Lord Curzon, the then Viceroy of India, and implemented on 16 October 1905, it was undone a mere six years later. Hence the correct answer is "1905".

q: Who was the Mughal emperor, when a massive rebellion against british rule broke out in 1857?
subject: history
exp: At the time that the Revolt of 1857 took place, Bahadur Shah Zafar was the emperor of the Mughal Empire. After being found guilty of treason by the British government, he was sentenced to exile in Rangoon in 1858, along with his wife Zeenat Mahal and other members of his extended family. On November 7, 1862, he passed away while living in exile. Hence, Bahadur Shah Zafar is the correct answer.

q: Who said the following words? "Sarfaroshi ki tamanna ab hamare dil me hai, dekhna hai zor kitna baazu-e-qaatil me hai"
subject: history
exp: Bismil Azimabadi of Patna is the author of the patriotic poetry Sarfaroshi ki Tamanna, which was composed in Urdu in the year 1921. During the time of British rule in India, it became famous as a call to arms for liberation that was popularised by Ram Prasad Bismil. - These are the last words that were said by Ram Prasad Bismil before he was executed by hanging. Hence, Ramprasad Bismil is the correct answer.

q: Which of the following Indian freedom fighters said that "Be the change you wish to see in the world"?
subject: history
exp: Mahatma Gandhi said that "Be the change you wish to see in the world". This is a famous quote that reflects Gandhiji's principle of non-violent resistance. Gandhiji believed that everyone can make a difference in the world around them, however small it may be. He encouraged people to recognize their strengths and bring positive changes in their lives. This quote of Gandhiji is as relevant today as when he first said it. It reminds us that we can all make a difference in the world around us, even if we are alone. Hence, the correct answer is "Mahatma Gandhi".

q: After the death of Shri Guru Gobind Singh, the Sikhs revolted against the ______ under the leadership of Banda Bahadur.
subject: history
exp: Banda Singh Bahadur led the Khalsa army and was a Sikh warrior. On the banks of the Godavari River, at Nanded, he founded a monastery. Following his baptism, Banda changed his name to Gurbaksh Singh after becoming a disciple of Guru Gobind Singh. After Guru Gobind Singh was killed, he started a rebellion against the Mughals. On June 9th, 1716, he was taken prisoner and put to death during the rule of Farrukh Siyar. Hence, the correct answer is "Mughals".

q: In which of the following years was the Dowry Prohibition Act passed in India?
subject: history
exp: On July 1, 1961, the Dowry Prohibition Act came into effect. Its goal was to forbid dowry giving and receiving. A "dowry" is any property or valuable security given or agreed to be given, directly or indirectly, as defined by the Dowry Prohibition Act. All Indian religions are covered by the Dowry Prohibition Act. Five years in prison and a fine of Rs. fifteen thousand rupees, or the equivalent of the dowry, are the penalties for breaking the law. Hence, the correct answer is "1961".

q: India lost a large proportion of _____ growing area to Pakistan during partition.
subject: history
exp: In semi-arid regions of the nation during the kharif season, cotton is a tropical crop farmed. During the partition, Pakistan gained a sizable piece of the land used by India for cotton cultivation. However, during the past 50 years, its acreage has grown significantly. India cultivates 'narma', or long-staple cotton, as well as short-staple (Indian) and long- staple (American) cotton in the northwest. Hence the correct answer is "cotton" .

q: In which of the following years did the resolution at the Karachi Session of the Indian National Congress dwell on how Independent India's Constitution should look?
subject: history
exp: The Indian National Congress' Karachi session passed a resolution in 1931 outlining the independent India's Constitution. leaders agreed on several fundamental principles long before the Constituent Assembly convened to discuss the Constitution, such as the right to adult franchise, the right to freedom, equality, and the protection of minorities' rights. The Karachi Congress was presided over by Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel. Hence, the correct answer is "1931".

q: In which of the following years did the Indian army free Goa from the Portuguese?
subject: history
exp: India gained independence from British rule in 1947, but Goa, a Portuguese colony at the time, took another 14 years to be free of foreign control. On December 19, 1961, Indian armed personnel seized control of Goa from the Portuguese, bringing an end to approximately 450 years of colonial rule. The Goa Opinion Poll was a referendum held on January 16, 1967, in the Indian state of Goa to determine the future of the Union Territories of Goa, Daman, and Diu. Hence, the correct answer is "1961".

q: Warren Hastings became the first Governor General of India in which year?
subject: history
exp: In 1773, Warren Hastings was appointed to his position as the first Governor- General of Bengal. He put an end to Robert Clive's Dual System, which had been in place since its inception. Hence, the correct answer is "1773"

q: 'Do or die' slogan is given by which of the following freedom fighter?
subject: history
exp: The "Do or Die" slogan was given by Mahatma Gandhi during the Quit India Movement launched on August 8, 1942. This movement was a call for Indians to demand an end to British rule and to actively participate in the struggle for India's independence. Hence, the correct answer is "Mahatma Gandhi".

q: On 21 October 1943, Subhash Chandra Bose proclaimed the formation of the Provisional Government of Free India in ______.
subject: history
exp: Subhash Chandra Bose announced the establishment of the Provisional Government of Free India in Singapore on October 21, 1943. It was also referred to as Free India or Azad Hind. Imperial Japan provided it with military, political, and financial support when it was founded. The Azad Hind government's army was known as the Indian National Army (INA). Hence, the correct answer is “Singapore".

q: The bill which provided for the trial of British or European persons by Indians was _____ .
subject: history
exp: The Ilbert Bill was a contentious piece of legislation that was introduced on February 9, 1883, when the Marquess of Ripon was serving as Viceroy. The purpose of the Bill was to open the door for senior Indian judges to sit over cases in India that included British nationals. Sir Courtenay Peregine Ilbert, who serves as a legal member on the Council of the Governor-General of India, was the one who authored the document. Hence, the correct answer is "Ilbert Bill ."

q: Chandannagar was established as a French colony in 1673 , obtaining permission from _______ the then Nawab of Bengal, to establish a trading post on the right bank of the river Hooghly.
subject: history
exp: One of the five French Indian colonies, Chandannagar was situated on the Hooghly River's western bank. By obtaining Ibrahim Khan, the then- Nawab of Bengal, as approval, the French East India company founded it as a trade base in 1673. In 1688, the area was settled permanently by French people. In 1730, Joseph Dupleix was appointed mayor. French and British forces were at war for control of the Indian subcontinent at the time. Hence the correct answer is "Ibrahim Khan".

q: Which of the following British officers issued the infamous 'crawling order' whereby Indians had to crawl on all fours to pass an alley?
subject: history
exp: The British government's "crawling order" was connected to the Jallianwala Bagh tragedy. The British Indian Army's General Reginald Dyer was the Brigadier who gave the order to soldiers to open fire on a mob of unarmed Indian people. It took place on April 13, 1919, in Amritsar, Punjab. Rabindranath Tagore was horrified by this episode and forfeited his knighthood. Hence the correct answer is "General Dyer".

q: The Poona Pact of 1932 was an agreement between Mahatma Gandhi and ______.
subject: history
exp: The agreement that Mahatma Gandhi and Dr. Bhimrao Ambedkar signed on September 24, 1932, is known as the Poona Pact. Gandhi's prison fast, which he had instituted to protest British Prime Minister Ramsay Macdonald's decision to grant the Depressed Classes their own electorate, came to an end with this Pact. Under Lord Willingdon's leadership, it was signed. The agreement made in 1932 to reserve electoral seats for the lower classes in the British India Parliament is known as the Poona Pact. At the Yerwada Central Jail in Pune, Madan Mohan Malviya signed it on behalf of B.R. Ambedkar and Mahatma Gandhi. Hence, the correct answer is "B.R. Ambedkar".

q: The Paramhans Mandali was founded in 1840 at ___________ to work for the abolition of caste.
subject: history
exp: In the year 1849, the city of Bombay became the home of a covert socio- religious organisation known as the Paramahansa Mandali. It was begun by Dadoba Pandurang, Durgaram Mehtaji, and a handful of Durgaram Mehtaji's other acquaintances. It was the first organisation of its kind in the Indian state of Maharashtra. Hence, the correct answer is 'Bombay'.

q: The first session of All India Depressed Classes congress was held at ____ in 1930.
subject: history
exp: Nagpur served as the location for the inaugural meeting of the All-India Depressed Classes Congress (AIDCC), which took place on August 8, 1930. B.R. Ambedkar, a prominent social reformer, jurist, and a key advocate for Dalit rights, played a pivotal role in organizing and leading the efforts of the All India Depressed Classes Congress. Hence, the correct answer is "Nagpur"

q: The Contempt of Courts Act was passed to define and limit the powers of certain courts in punishing contempt of courts and to regulate their procedure in relation thereto. In which of the following years was the Act passed?
subject: history
exp: In 1971, the Contempt of Courts Act was adopted. An Act to Regulate the Procedure in Relation to Punishing Contempt of Courts and to Define and Limit the Powers of Certain Courts. The contempt of court is given by ‘wilful disobedience to any judgment, decree, direction, order, writ or other process of a court or wilful breach of an undertaking given to a court". The Indian Parliament enacted this Act in 1971 with the intention of defining what constitutes contempt of court. This indicates that the court's exercise of contempt jurisdiction is limited to preserving the integrity of the current legal system. Hence, the correct answer is "1971".

q: Vasco da Gama, a Portuguese explorer, discovered the sea route to India in____.
subject: history
exp: On May 20, 1498, two years after setting sail from Lisbon, Portugal, Vasco da Gama landed on the Western sea coast of India at Kozhikode (Calicut), Kerala. This marked the beginning of his exploration of the Indian subcontinent. This was the first time that a European had travelled to India by way of the water. Hence, the correct answer is "1498"

q: What was the age in years of the revolutionary Bhagat Singh when he was hanged till death?
subject: history
exp: Bhagat Singh was 23 years old when he was executed. On 23rd March 1931, Bhagat Singh, Raj Guru, and Sukhdev were hanged to death. In connection with the Lahore conspiracy case, they received the death penalty. Denying the accused the right to appeal, the British Governor-General issued an ordinance creating a special tribunal to try the Lahore conspiracy case. India celebrates "Martyr's Day," also known as "Shaheed Diwas," on March 23 of each year as a memorial to the three revolutionaries who were hanged by the British: Bhagat Singh, Shivaram Rajguru, and Sukhdev Thapar. On September 27, 1907, Bhagat Singh was born in Punjab, India (now Pakistan), into a Sikh family that was heavily involved in politics. Hence, the correct answer is "23".

q: Based on Macaulay's Minute, the English Education Act was introduced in ____ .
subject: history
exp: Thomas Babington Macaulay delivered his "Minute on Indian Education" on February 2, 1835. The purpose of this speech was to argue for the necessity of providing "natives" of India with an education in the English language. The report of Macaulay's Minute served as the foundation for the English Education Act of 1835. Hence, the correct answer is "1835."

q: The _________ considers a marriage as complete and binding at the completion of the Saptapadi ritual.
subject: history
exp: Section 7 in The Hindu Marriage Act, 1955 Hindus are free to marry in line with their own cultures' traditional rituals and ceremonies. These rituals and ceremonies include the saptpadi, which has the bridegroom and bride walking seven steps together in front of a holy fire. The marriage is considered to be final and binding after the seventh step is taken. Hence the correct answer is "The Hindu Marriage Act, 1955".

q: The Paramhans Mandali was established to work for the abolition of caste in ______.
subject: history
exp: Established in Bombay, Paramhans Mandali was a significant and powerful Hindu socio-religious organization. 'Dadoba Pandrung' founded this organization. In 1840 (1849), Paramhans Mandali was founded to promote the abolition of caste. It operated as a covert social-radical society at first. The fundamental tenet of the Samaj was that genuine religion is founded on love and moral behavior, and that only God should be worshipped. All the members of this congregation were exhorted to abandon caste prejudice. Hence, the correct answer is "Bombay".

q: Raja Ram Mohan Roy founded a reform association known as Brahmo Sabha which was later known as ______.
subject: history
exp: In the year 1828, Raja Ram Mohan Roy established the Brahmo Sabha, which would eventually become known as the Brahmo Samaj. Its primary objective was the worship of the everlasting God, and it was opposed to the institution of priesthood as well as rites and sacrifices. It was thought that all faiths should work together to create a better world and put an emphasis on prayer, meditation, and the study of scripture. Hence, the correct answer is "Brahmo Samaj".

q: Who among the following Indian freedom fighters was fondly called Gandhi Buri (Ōld Lady Gandhi) for her dedication towards Gandhian principles?
subject: history
exp: An Indian revolutionary named Matangini Hazra took part in the fight for Indian independence. In front of the Tamluk Police Station in the Midnapore District, she was fatally shot by British Indian police. The names "Gandhi buri" and "Bangla for Old Lady Gandhi" were given to her with fondness. She participated in the Non-Cooperation Movement in 1920 and was detained for violating the Salt Act. She also took part in the campaign to abolish the chowkidari tax, or "Chowkidari Tax Bandha." Hence the correct answer is "Matangini Hazra".

q: The Ramakrishna Mission stressed the ideal of __________ through social service and selfless action.
subject: history
exp: On May 1, 1897, Swami Vivekananda founded the Ramakrishna Mission with the purpose of emphasising the values of salvation by social service and the action of giving without expecting anything in return. His spiritual guide and patron, Ramakrishna Paramahamsa, was the source of both the mission's name and its inspiration. Mystic Ramakrishna Paramahamsa was said to have resided and worshipped at the Dakshineshwar temple during his lifetime. Hence, the correct answer is "Salvation".

q: Vernacular press Act was enacted in which year?
subject: history
exp: The Vernacular Press Act (1878) was enacted by British India in an effort to limit press freedom and quell opposition to British policies, particularly that which had grown since the start of the Second Anglo-Afghan War. The Viceroy's Council unanimously adopted the Act on March 14, 1878, after Lytton, the Viceroy of India at the time, submitted it. The Vernacular Press Act (VPA) was created to "better control" the vernacular press and to criminalize and outlaw "seditious literature" in "publications in oriental languages." Consequently, the British had nothing but animosity towards the Indian press, which did not speak English. Hence, the correct answer is "1878".

q: When did the Indian Independence Act receive the British royal assent?
subject: history
exp: The Indian Independence Act of 1947 is a piece of legislation that was passed by the Parliament of the United Kingdom and is responsible for the division of British India into the two new sovereign dominions of India and Pakistan. The Act was given the Royal Assent on July 18, 1947; hence, modern-day India and Pakistan came into existence on August 15, 1947, with the west becoming Pakistan and the east becoming Bangladesh.Finally the transfer of power took place. Hence, the correct answer is " July 1947 ."

q: Which of the following was a fishing colony that was used as a port for trading with the Romans and the Greco-Romans in ancient India?
subject: history
exp: In Pondicherry, Arikamedu was a historic Roman trading hub and coastal community. Between 200 BC and 200 AD, the fishing colony of Arikamedu served as a port for trade with Greco-Roman and Roman traders. Here too, there is evidence of inland trade along the river valley routes with other megalithic sites such as Musiri, Suttukeni, Kaveripattinam, and Alagankulam. Situated on the banks of the Bhogava River, a right tributary of the Sabarmati River, lies the Gujarati port city of Lothal. Pulakeshin governed from Badami, the Chalukyan capital and the principal city south of the Narmada River. Hence, the correct answer is "Arikamedu".

q: Which one of the following is connected with the ‘Blue Water’ policy?
subject: history
exp: The ‘Blue Water’ policy is attributed to Francisco de Almeida. He was the first Viceroy of Portuguese possessions in India. According to it, “The Portuguese should be the sole trade power in the Arabian Sea and the Indian Ocean”. Hence, the correct answer is “Francisco de Almeida”.

q: The Hindu college was established in 1791 at ______.
subject: history
exp: The Hindu college was founded in 1791 with the goal of promoting the study of Sanskrit literature from ancient times. The Sanskrit College was founded by Governor General Lord Cornwallis and Sri Jonathan Duncan. Jonathan Duncan proposed the creation of a Sanskrit college to support Indian education in Britain by cultivating and maintaining Sanskrit Vangmaya. The Sanskrit college was elevated to the status of a university in 1958, and the name was changed to Sampurnanand Sanskrit University in 1974. On March 22, 1958, Dr. Sampurnanand, the then-chief minister, and Pt. Kamlapati Tripathi, the minister of education, established the university in Varanasi. Hence, the correct answer is "Banaras".

q: Which of the following events occurred before 1919?
subject: history
exp: Lord Curzon, the viceroy of India at the time, declared the partition of Bengal on July 20 and it officially began on October 16 of that same year. Mohandas K. Gandhi, the head of the Indian nationalist movement, and Lord Irwin agreed to a deal on March 5, 1931. The Chauri Chaura event happened on February 4, 1922, in Chauri Chaura, United Provinces (modern-day Uttar Pradesh), in the Gorakhpur district. The 1929 Congress meeting was held in Lahore. Because the influential party Indian National Congress adopted the resolution of Poorna Swaraj or total independence, this Lahore session was particularly significant. Hence, the correct answer is "Partition of Bengal".

q: In which of the following years was the Indian National Congress split into two groups named as Moderates and Extremists?
subject: history
exp: In 1907, the session of the Indian National Congress was held in Surat, in which the Congress was divided into two parties called the Extremist Party and the Moderate Dal. This is called the Surat division. The session of 1907 was presided over by Rash Behari Ghosh. Gopal Krishna Gokhale, Pherozeshah Mehta, and Surendranath Banerjee were in charge of the moderates, while Lokmanya Tilak, Lajpat Rai, and Sri Aurobindo were in charge of the extremists during this time period. Hence, the correct answer is "1907".

q: Who among the following revolutionaries was associated with Swadesh Bandhav Samiti?
subject: history
exp: After the partition of Bengal in 1905, Ashwini Kumar Dutta established the Swadesh Bandhab Samiti on August 6, 1905. The organization was founded in Barisal (present-day Bangladesh) as part of the wider Swadeshi Movement against British colonial policies. The primary objective of the Samiti was to promote the use of swadeshi (Indian- made) goods and to discourage the use of foreign products, especially British goods. It played an important role in spreading nationalist ideas among the masses, particularly in rural areas, through education, awareness campaigns, and social work. The Samiti also worked towards improving local self-reliance by encouraging indigenous industries and economic independence. It organized volunteer groups, set up schools, and promoted national education to strengthen the spirit of nationalism. Through its activities, the Swadesh Bandhab Samiti became one of the most influential organizations of the Swadeshi Movement in Bengal. Hence, the correct answer is "Ashwini Kumar Dutta."

q: Which of the following parties supported the move for partition of Bengal?
subject: history
exp: The partition of Bengal was advocated by the All-India Muslim League in 1905. The Viceroy of India from 1899 to 1905 was Lord Curzon. On October 16, 1905, during his viceroyalty, the province of Bengal was divided. The idea of using the Bengal partition as a political tool to undermine the growing nationalism in Bengal. According to Curzon, Bengal (which includes the present-day West Bengal, Odisha, and Bihar) and Eastern Bengal and Assam would be the two provinces following the partition. The majority population in Bengal would be Hindu, while that in Eastern Bengal and Assam would be Muslim. Its capital would remain Calcutta. Hence, the correct answer is "All India Muslim League".

q: Against whom was the Battle of Plassey (1757) fought by the English Company led by Robert Clive?
subject: history
exp: The Battle of Plassey was fought on 23 June 1757 between the British East India Company led by Robert Clive and Siraj ud Daulah, the Nawab of Bengal. The battle is considered a historic victory for the British and marked the beginning of British rule in India. Other options are faults. The Nizam of Awadh Murshid Quli Khan and the Shan Nawabs were not involved in the Battle of Plassey. Hence, the correct answer is “Siraj Ud Daulah”.

q: Which of the following revolutionaries was arrested by the British as an accused in the Alipore Bomb Conspiracy Case?
subject: history
exp: The Alipore Conspiracy case, 1908: This criminal case took place in India in 1908 and was also known as the Muraripukur conspiracy or the Manicktolla bomb conspiracy. Several Indian nationalists from the Anushilan Samiti were put on trial in Calcutta for allegedly "waging war against the Government" of the British Raj. The British detained Kanailal Dutta as a suspect in the Alipore Bomb Conspiracy Case. He was a revolutionary from the Jugantar group who fought for India's independence. He was born in West Bengal, in Chandannagar. Hence, the correct answer is "Kanailal Dutta".

q: Shyamji Krishna Varma established India House at _________ in 1905.
subject: history
exp: In 1905, Shyamji Krishna Varma founded the India House in London. India House was a student apartment building located on Cromwell Avenue in North London's Highgate. Journalist, attorney, and Indian patriot Shyamji Krishna Varma. India House, The Indian Sociologist, and the Indian Home Rule Society were all founded by him in London. In 1905, he established The Indian Sociologist and India House. An Indian nationalist publication was The Indian Sociologist. On February 18, 1905, Shyamji gave the Indian Home Rule Society its official launch. Hence, the correct answer is "London".

q: The proposed Rowlett Act of 1919 allowed the detention of political prisoners without trial for ______.
subject: history
exp: There was a law in British India called the Rowlatt Act. A legislative council statute was passed by the Imperial Legislative Council in Delhi on March 18, 1919. Passed in the midst of World War I, the Defence of India Act of 1915 extended the emergency provisions of preventative indefinite detention, imprisonment without trial, and indefinite judicial review. The recommendations of the Rowlatt Committee were accepted, and the committee was renamed in honor of its chairman, Sir Sidney Rowlatt. The act essentially gave the colonial British government permission to detain for up to two years anyone living in British India who was suspected of being a terrorist. It bestowed upon the colonial authorities the authority to handle any revolutionary activity. Hence, the correct answer is "two years".

q: Which of the following legislations ought to allow senior Indian magistrates to preside over cases involving British subjects in India?
subject: history
exp: Senior Indian magistrates should be able to oversee cases involving British subjects in India owing to the Ilbert Bill of 1884. Sir Courtenay Peregrine Ilbert, the legal member of the Council of the Governor- General of India, drafted the Ilbert Bill, which was introduced on February 9, 1883, during the Viceroyship of Lord Ripon. It was concerned about the jurisdiction of Magistrates or Sessions Judges to try charges against European British subjects if they were themselves not European. Before the bill was finally passed into law in 1884 in a seriously compromised state, it faced fierce opposition in Britain and from British settlers in India. This ultimately aggravated racial tensions. The Indian National Congress was formed in the wake of this contentious bill. Hence, the correct answer is "Ilbert Bill, 1884".

q: Who among the following leaders of Indian Freedom Movement gave the slogan ‘Freedom is my birthright and I shall have it’?
subject: history
exp: Bal Gangadhar Tilak (Lokmanya Tilak), who is regarded as the Indian independence movement's founding leader, coined the phrase "Swaraj is my birthright and I shall have it." In 1914, he established the Indian Home Rule League and served as its first President. In 1916, he and Mohammed Ali Jinnah signed the Lucknow Pact, which called for Hindu and Muslim solidarity in the nationalist cause. Hence, the correct answer is "Bal Gangadhar Tilak".

q: The first session of the Indian National Congress was held at ______.
subject: history
exp: From the 28th to the 31st of December in 1885, Bombay played host to the First Session of the Indian National Congress. Womesh Chandra Banerjee, a well- known Bengali lawyer, presided over the meeting as its chairperson. At the meeting, there were a total of 72 delegates. The Indian National Congress played a pivotal role in the Indian independence movement against British colonial rule. The party's early leaders included prominent figures like Allan Octavian Hume and Dadabhai Naoroji. Hence, the correct answer is "Bombay".

q: Which of the following has discovered the sea route to India (1498)?
subject: history
exp: Vasco da Gama was a Portuguese explorer who arrived in India in1498, after travelling to the continent by sea. Portugal's Vasco da Gama set off from Lisbon in 1497 to reach India and establish a sea route from Europe to the East. Vasco da Gama discovered the sea passage to India in 1498. His mission sailed down Africa's western coast and around the Cape of Good Hope, making several stops along the way, until landing in the commercial port of Calicut, India, in May 1498. Hence, the correct answer is "Vasco Da Gama"

q: In 1498, Vasco da Gama, a Portuguese explorer had discovered a route to __________.
subject: history
exp: In 1498, Vasco da Gama, a Portuguese explorer, discovered the sea route to India via the Cape of Good Hope. He landed at Calicut (now Kozhikode), Kerala, marking the beginning of European sea trade with India. This discovery opened direct maritime trade between Europe and India. Hence, the correct answer is India.

q: Battle of Buxar was fought in _______.
subject: history
exp: The Battle of Buxar was fought on October 22, 1764 AD. The Mughal emperor Shah Alam II, the British, and the joint armies of Nawab Mir Qasim of Bengal, and Nawab Sujauddaula of Awadh, participated in the battle. On October 22, 1764, the United Indian Army was vanquished in the Battle of Buxar. Sujauddaulah, Shah Alam, and Company Governor Clive signed a treaty in Allahabad in 1765. The company received civil rights under this treaty in Bengal, Bihar, and Orissa, allowing it to collect revenue from these regions. Hence, the correct answer is "1764".

q: In which of the following years was the Mont- Ford Reforms report presented by the British colonial government in India?
subject: history
exp: Montagu-Chelmsford Report was formed on the basis of the Government of India Act 1919, was published on 8th July 1918. British Government has appointed Edwin Montagu as the Secretary of State for India in 1917 and remained in that office till 1922. He brought out a detailed report titled Constitutional Reforms in India, along with the Governor-General of India Lord Chelmsford, The report is also called the Montagu- Chelmsford Report. Hence, "1918" is the correct answer.

q: Ryotwari system of revenue collection in India, introduced by the British, was based on the _______.
subject: history
exp: The Ryotwari system of revenue collection in India, introduced by the British, was based on the:Ricardian theory of rent The Ryotwari system of revenue collection was introduced by the British in India during their colonial rule. This system was designed to collect land revenue directly from individual farmers or "ryots." The average income from different types of soil was estimated, the revenue-paying capacity of the ryot was assessed and a proportion of it fixed as the share of the state. The lands were resurveyed every 30 years and the revenue rates increased. Therefore the revenue demand was no longer permanent. The system was based on the principles of the Ricardian theory of rent, which was formulated by the British economist David Ricardo. Hence the correct answer is "Ricardian theory of rent".

q: With which of the following Government of India Acts did the Montagu-Chelmsford Reforms introduce Dyarchy in the provinces?
subject: history
exp: The Montagu–Chelmsford Reforms, also known as the Government of India Act 1919, were a major reform of the British Indian government. They brought in a dual system where some matters were overseen by Indian ministers under provincial legislatures, and others by British officials under the Governor. This aimed to meet Indian demands for self-rule. The plan faced challenges, as it was tough to categorize subjects for administration. Indian ministers had limited power, while British officials were hesitant to yield authority. The system ended in 1935 with the Government of India Act, which centralized governance. The reforms marked a step toward self- governance and paved the way for later changes. Hence, the correct answer is "Government of India Act, 1919".

q: Who among the following was the President of the Indian National Congress during the 1915 session?
subject: history
exp: Satyendra Prasanna Sinha served as the President of the Indian National Congress during its session in 1915 held in Bombay. He was a distinguished lawyer and a prominent statesman in British India, known for his contributions to Indian politics and law. Sinha was the first Indian to join the Executive Council of the Viceroy as a result of the Indian Councils Act of 1909, marking a significant step for Indian representation in governance. He also held several important positions, including being the first Governor of Bihar and Orissa and the first Indian Advocate-General of Bengal. Sinha's leadership in the Congress during this period was vital as it represented a time of growing political awareness and activism among Indians. Hence, the correct answer is "Satyendra Prasanna Sinha."

q: In 1942, the British government sent the ______ mission to India.
subject: history
exp: The Cripps Mission was a significant political initiative sent by the British government to India during World War II. It was led by Sir Stafford Cripps, a senior British politician. The mission arrived in India in March 1942 and was dispatched with the primary goal of seeking Indian cooperation and support for the British war effort, particularly in the context of the global conflict. Hence, the correct answer is "Cripps".

q: Who was the first Indian to qualify for Covenanted service?
subject: history
exp: Civil Service Commission was set up in 1854 in London and competitive examinations were started in 1855. Initially, the examinations for the Indian Civil Service were conducted only in London. The maximum age was 23 years and the minimum age was 18 years. The syllabus was designed such that European Classics had a predominant share of marks. All this made it difficult for Indian candidates. Nevertheless, in 1864, the first Indian, Shri Satyendranath Tagore, the brother of Shri Rabindranath Tagore succeeded. Hence, the correct answer is "Satyendra Nath Tagore".

q: Self-Government or 'Swaraj' as the ultimate goal of the Indian National Congress-this declaration was made by Dadabhai Naoroji in which of the following sessions of the INC?
subject: history
exp: Dadabhai Naoroji declared Self- Government or 'Swaraj' as the ultimate goal of the Indian National Congress in the Calcutta session of 1906. This was a major turning point in the Indian independence movement, as it marked the first time that the Congress had officially adopted a goal of complete independence from British rule. Naoroji was a prominent leader of the Indian independence movement and is considered to be the "Grand Old Man of India". He was a strong advocate of Swaraj, or self-government, and believed that it was the only way to achieve true freedom for India. Naoroji's declaration of Swaraj as the goal of the INC was a significant step in the Indian independence movement and helped to inspire millions of Indians to join the struggle for freedom. Hence, the correct answer is "Calcutta".

q: The Champaran Satyagraha took place in _____ .
subject: history
exp: The Champaran Satyagraha was the first major Satyagraha movement launched by Mahatma Gandhi in India. The movement was launched in Champaran district of Bihar, where the indigo planters had been forcing the peasants to grow indigo on 3/20th of their land. The peasants were forced to do this even if the indigo crop failed. Gandhi went to Champaran in April 1917, and he met with the peasants and listened to their grievances.
